expectations.text: I thought this would be easier the third time around.
I'm hoping for the most basic thing: that we both walk out of that hospital okay.

perceptions.text: The nurses are kind, don't get me wrong. But I can see it in their eyes. They're wondering how I'm going to manage.
My doctors have been thorough about the pre-eclampsia diagnosis. They explain things well—probably because they know I work in healthcare.

valued_items.text: The doctors have been pretty thorough about [pain management options], which I appreciate.
The hospital is wonderful about financial assistance programs

missing_items.text: They manage my blood pressure and my symptoms, but they don't manage me. The fear, the isolation, the financial stress
Nobody's really addressing how I'm going to manage with three kids and limited income when I'm potentially dealing with complications.

service_gaps.expectation: Needs holistic support acknowledging fear and isolation.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a clinical case to be managed rather than a person.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrate psychosocial screening into high-risk prenatal appointments.

service_gaps.expectation: Clear plan for logistical survival while in high-risk care.
service_gaps.perception: Financial assistance exists, but lacks practical coordination for single parents.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Dedicated patient navigator for high-risk single parents to assist with childcare/logistics.

innovation_opportunities.description: Integrated 'Patient Navigator' role for high-risk, single-parent patients to assist with logistical and childcare planning.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Nobody's really addressing how I'm going to manage with three kids and limited income when I'm potentially dealing with complications.

innovation_opportunities.description: Standardized Psychosocial Screening tool that triggers referral to mental health and social support services during high-risk diagnosis.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They manage my blood pressure and my symptoms, but they don't manage me.

expectations.text: I just wanted to feel safe. Supported. Like someone was actually listening when I said I was scared instead of just checking boxes on a form.
I wish someone would sit down with me and help me figure out what's realistic and what I actually need, not just what looks best on paper.

perceptions.text: It's all about managing the blood pressure and the protein levels. The clinical stuff. Not the person behind the numbers.
When I go to appointments, I can see the shift in how people treat me once they realize there's no partner. It's subtle, but it's there. Like I'm already a cautionary tale.

valued_items.text: She asked how I was doing, and not in that automatic way. Like, she actually waited for an answer. When I admitted I was terrified, she didn't try to minimize it.

missing_items.text: Nobody's really asked me about my support system or helped me plan for that reality.
I wish someone had screened me for depression and anxiety from the beginning, not just monitored my blood pressure.

service_gaps.expectation: Feeling listened to and having fears validated.
service_gaps.perception: Clinical interactions focus exclusively on numerical data and protocol compliance.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement structured 'whole-person' check-ins during appointments.

service_gaps.expectation: Integrated support for non-medical barriers like childcare and financial stress.
service_gaps.perception: Disconnected silos where social/financial needs are ignored or left to the patient to navigate.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Embed social work/resource navigators within the high-risk maternity clinic.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'Continuity of Care' advocate role that acts as a consistent point of contact for high-risk, single mothers throughout the journey.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: It would be continuity of care. Having one person... who knows me, who checks in consistently, who sees me as a whole person.

innovation_opportunities.description: Digital 'Support Needs Assessment' tool to identify social, financial, and emotional risks early and route them to automated or human support resources.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Nobody's really asked me about my support system or helped me plan for that reality.

expectations.text: I wanted a doctor to take that seriously, not move on because my chart said 'low risk.'
I want care that speaks my language — not just an interpreter on the phone but someone who slows down, uses plain words, and looks at me like I am a whole person.

perceptions.text: The blank line where 'marital status' goes made me feel judged
I wish someone had asked me to tell my story, not just check boxes.

valued_items.text: I am proud of my strength — I want providers to see that, to trust me when I say something hurts or feels wrong.

missing_items.text: help with the practical parts: paperwork for work, referrals, a support group with other immigrant moms.

service_gaps.expectation: Providers to listen to history of chronic pain and not dismiss based on 'low risk' labels.
service_gaps.perception: Clinical interaction focused on check-boxes and standard protocols over personal narrative.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'Narrative Medicine' templates in EHR that prioritize patient-reported history over binary checkboxes.

service_gaps.expectation: Culturally sensitive, inclusive, and human-centered communication.
service_gaps.perception: Use of exclusionary forms and reliance on tele-interpreters without personal rapport.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Develop inclusive registration forms that remove stigmatizing fields; mandate communication training for clinicians focused on cultural humility.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a 'Patient Story' digital module within the maternity record where patients can upload their health history and goals in their own words.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wish someone had asked me to tell my story, not just check boxes.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish culturally tailored post-birth support programs led by peer navigators for immigrant mothers.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: help with the practical parts: paperwork for work, referrals, a support group with other immigrant moms.

expectations.text: I wanted a vaginal delivery, minimal intervention if possible.
I thought I had this all figured out. I've spent my career being the competent one... the expert.

perceptions.text: The OB is... complicated. She's excellent, truly. But there's this professional distance between us that I created.
It's clinical and real and a little bit scary.

valued_items.text: He just met me where I was—terrified and vulnerable and nothing like the competent woman he married.
One of them earlier just held space, didn't hover.

missing_items.text: Part of me wanted her to just... hold my hand and tell me it would be okay. Not explain the science of it.
What I wish someone had told me... is that knowledge doesn't prepare you for vulnerability.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional, informed, and controlled birth experience.
service_gaps.perception: Professional distance prevents human connection and clinical environment feels isolating.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Redesign OB-colleague interactions to prioritize 'patient' over 'peer' status.

innovation_opportunities.description: Provider-Patient Role-Switch Training: Develop communication protocols for professionals treating colleagues to ensure the 'patient' persona is validated and nurtured.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: She treats me like a colleague even now... part of me is screaming, 'Just be my doctor.'

innovation_opportunities.description: Liminal Space Support Toolkit: Tools for clinical staff to identify and support 'professional' patients who are experiencing high vulnerability and loss of control.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Maternity care services need to understand that the most competent, educated women can fall apart in labor.

expectations.text: I wanted a natural birth — no epidural, minimal intervention.
I had this almost idealistic vision of myself breathing through contractions, staying present, maybe even feeling this transcendent connection to motherhood.

perceptions.text: My OB definitely tried. But there's this awkwardness when you're treating a colleague... I could feel her hesitation.
She [the nurse] was just looking at me like I was a woman in labor. She came over and put her hand on my back and said, 'You're doing this. Your body knows what to do.'

valued_items.text: That respect for my intelligence without assuming I need to be in control — that's been grounding.
My husband has been incredible — he's the only person I can really be fully myself with right now.

missing_items.text: Nobody explicitly gave me permission to stop being the expert and just be the patient.
I needed her clinical judgment, her gut feeling as someone who knows my body and this situation.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional-patient equality and shared decision making based on expertise.
service_gaps.perception: OB acting as if on eggshells, withholding clinical advice due to colleague status.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Standardized 'provider-as-patient' care protocol to bypass collegial awkwardness.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a 'Provider-Patient' care pathway that includes an explicit 'Surrender-to-Patient' permission protocol.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Nobody explicitly gave me permission to stop being the expert and just be the patient.

innovation_opportunities.description: Redesign clinical spaces to offer 'human-centered' rather than 'techno-centric' zones for low-risk labor phases, even within hospitals.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wish the room felt less like a medical space and more like... I don't know, somewhere human.

expectations.text: I wanted staff to ask about that [past hurt], not just my blood pressure — to offer trauma-informed care, not assumptions.
My biggest hope is that future care treats people as whole people — with histories, fears, and strengths.

perceptions.text: a midwife who sat down and actually listened, the nurse who dimmed the lights when I asked. Those moments reminded me I wasn’t just a “low-risk chart.”
And please stop acting like young, single parents are less capable; respect matters.

valued_items.text: a midwife who sat down and actually listened
the nurse who dimmed the lights when I asked

missing_items.text: Mental health support needs to be real and easy to get, not a pamphlet shoved in a discharge folder.
I wish there was more practical help for someone my age — help finding steady work, affordable childcare, and home visits after birth.

service_gaps.expectation: Staff to ask about past hurt and provide trauma-informed care.
service_gaps.perception: Staff primarily focused on prenatal numbers/blood pressure.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrate trauma-informed screening tools into standard intake procedures.

service_gaps.expectation: Respect for capacity as a young, single parent.
service_gaps.perception: Experienced assumptions of incapability based on demographic status.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Bias-mitigation and cultural sensitivity training for maternity staff regarding young parents.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implementation of a 'Trauma-Informed Care' intake process that prioritizes social and emotional history over clinical metrics alone.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wanted staff to ask about that [past hurt]... to offer trauma-informed care, not assumptions.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a holistic postnatal navigation hub connecting young parents to financial, employment, and childcare resources alongside mental health support.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wish there was more practical help for someone my age — help finding steady work, affordable childcare, and home visits after birth.

expectations.text: I wanted that classic, supportive midwife relationship you read about—someone who would remember my name, someone who would sit with me and actually listen to the fears I have about doing this solo.
I wanted to feel like I had a teammate in all of this.

perceptions.text: My first visit felt very fast. They checked my vitals, took my history, and because I’m 'low-risk,' the whole thing felt very routine—like I was just another line item.
Hearing that heartbeat for the first time? That was the only moment I’ve felt truly connected to the person I’m becoming.

valued_items.text: Hearing that heartbeat for the first time? That was the only moment I’ve felt truly connected to the person I’m becoming.
I’m proud I did it [teaching myself], but it shouldn't be that hard.

missing_items.text: I didn't feel like I could ask them about the anxiety or the loneliness, because that’s not really on their checklists, is it?
I wish there had been more space in those initial appointments to just be a person, not a patient.

service_gaps.expectation: Expectation of a supportive, listening partner relationship with midwife.
service_gaps.perception: Midwife sees her as a 'low-risk' routine case and is often rushed or clinical.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implementing a 'relationship-based' continuity model for solo parents.

service_gaps.expectation: Needs to discuss mental well-being and loneliness.
service_gaps.perception: Appointment focus is strictly on vitals and checklists, leaving no room for emotional needs.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrating psychosocial screening/support into every routine prenatal visit.

innovation_opportunities.description: Dedicated 'Solo Parent' navigation support to address the lack of a personal 'village' during the pregnancy journey.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Who do I call if I’m exhausted beyond belief, or if I just need to hear that I’m doing a good job?

innovation_opportunities.description: Digital 'Transition-to-Parenting' tool that offers clinical, social, and emotional checklists specifically for solo/single expectant mothers.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: If I can tick every box, maybe I can convince myself that I’m actually capable of doing this.

expectations.text: I just need someone to acknowledge that even if the medicine is easy, the journey is really heavy.
I want a space where I could say, 'I’m scared of the nights alone,' and have someone meet that with, 'I hear you, and here is how we can support you.'
I really wish there was someone—a counselor or even a midwife with time—to sit with me and say, 'You don't have to choose the most intense monitoring just because you’re scared.'

perceptions.text: When I walk into that clinic in Fall River, I feel like I’m in a high-speed lane—vitals, weight, blood pressure, 'everything looks good, see you in four weeks.'

She looked up and said, 'Dian, you’re doing this all on your own. How are you managing the pressure of that?' ...That was the moment I realized that 'good care' isn't about more time; it’s about a human connection.
When I pulled out my notebook to read them, the provider... actually chuckled... He said, 'You first-time moms are all...'

valued_items.text: That heartbeat on the monitor is a high that sustains me for days.
It was the moment I realized that 'good care' isn't about more time; it’s about a human connection.

missing_items.text: I wish there was a way to weave that emotional check-in into the routine.
The obstacle isn't that I don't have access to care; it’s that the care feels transactional.

service_gaps.expectation: Needs emotional validation for the challenges of single parenthood.
service_gaps.perception: Transactional, 'vitals-only' care that ignores the person behind the data.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integration of psychosocial screening into routine clinical intake.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional support in navigating decision-making and anxiety.
service_gaps.perception: Dismissive attitude ('chuckling') when patient attempts to advocate or ask questions.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implementation of patient-led 'question prompt lists' that clinicians are trained to acknowledge.

innovation_opportunities.description: Routine psychosocial 'emotional vital signs' check-ins during every appointment.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: If someone asked me about my anxieties with the same casual tone they use to ask about my morning sickness, it would be a total game-changer.

innovation_opportunities.description: Provider-facilitated 'Bridge' resources connecting single mothers to peer networks or tailored support groups.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want the medical team to just *be* that bridge—to offer the clinical expertise *with* the kind of compassion that says, 'I understand why you're scared, and you aren't doing this alone.'

expectations.text: I thought I'd be calmer about it all — I understand the science, I know the statistics... I thought I could do pregnancy well.

perceptions.text: My OB knows my background, which is helpful in some ways but also makes our appointments feel less like normal prenatal care and more like peer consultations.
The appointment felt... I don't know, efficient? ...Which is useful, but it's cold.

valued_items.text: She reviewed my history — no major complications with my first two pregnancies... She was reassuring but not dismissive.
There's continuity there, she knows my history, my quirks, my background.

missing_items.text: What I needed was someone to acknowledge that knowing too much about pregnancy risks is its own kind of burden.
I wanted someone to tell me it would be okay. Not just statistically likely, but actually okay.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected a supportive environment where she could be vulnerable as a patient.
service_gaps.perception: Professional-to-professional peer consultation dynamic.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Provider-patient interaction tools that allow for 'vulnerability check-ins' despite professional background.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a 'Maternal Mental Health/Anxiety' screening tool specifically for high-literacy/clinical patients to bypass the 'professional mask'.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I've made it hard for anyone to really care for me because I keep presenting as someone who doesn't need it.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement 'Non-Clinical' touchpoints (e.g., midwifery-led sessions) designed to pivot from data-heavy consultations to human-centered emotional support.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wonder if a midwife's approach might be different... Someone who could normalize the fear instead of intellectualizing it.

expectations.text: I think what I wanted most was permission to just be a patient. Not a colleague. Not someone who already knows the answer. Just... someone carrying a baby who needs reassurance.
I wanted continuity too. Someone who knew my whole story, not just my chart.

perceptions.text: My OB is wonderful, don't get me wrong, but our dynamic is complicated by what I do. [...] We were speaking the same language, which should be good, but it meant I never got to just hear, 'Your baby is fine. Relax.'
I'm seeing whoever's available. It's fragmented.

valued_items.text: When she says, 'This is what the evidence shows, but here's what I've seen clinically,' I trust that more than when she just gives me a reassurance without backing it up.
If someone had said, 'I see you're afraid. That's okay. But here's what the evidence actually shows... and here's why you can breathe.'

missing_items.text: No one asked how it was affecting me psychologically. No one acknowledged that the physical changes might be triggering something deeper about aging, identity, control.
I think what's missing is someone who can say, 'Your knowledge is real and valid, AND your anxiety is disproportionate to your actual risk.'

service_gaps.expectation: To be treated as a patient needing emotional reassurance.
service_gaps.perception: Professional relationship creates distance; OB defaults to clinical data/protocols because of participant's background.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Develop a 'High-Knowledge Patient' care pathway that separates clinical briefing from emotional support sessions.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a specialized 'Patient-Provider Persona Matching' or screening tool to identify high-health-literacy patients who need specialized psychological grounding rather than purely clinical reassurance.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I'm not most pregnant people.

innovation_opportunities.description: Integration of 'Reflective Care Rounds' where clinicians can document not just clinical findings, but also the patient's emotional state regarding their health literacy/anxiety levels.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: My OB isn't trained to handle a patient whose anxiety is rooted in understanding the actual science.

expectations.text: I wanted a partnership. I think I dreamed of someone who would look past the 'advanced maternal age' tag and actually see me—a human being who was coming to this experience with a history of trauma and loss.
I thought if I just did everything 'right'—if I followed the science to the letter—I could earn a healthy pregnancy.

perceptions.text: They see the 'medium risk' label, and they start ticking boxes... It felt like I was being processed.
I’m drowning in information, but I’m starving for someone to just ask me how I am.

valued_items.text: I wanted someone who would say, 'I know you’re terrified, and that’s a valid way to feel,' rather than just reciting the standard deviation risks.

missing_items.text: I didn't have a model for how to be vulnerable with a pregnancy... I don’t have a script for how to be held or supported.
When I’m nauseous... having to stand at a bus stop in the cold for forty minutes to get to a routine appointment feels like a monumental task.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: having to stand at a bus stop in the cold for forty minutes to get to a routine appointment feels like a monumental task.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’m drowning in information, but I’m starving for someone to just ask me how I am.

innovation_opportunities.description: Peer-support matching tool specifically for patients navigating 'advanced maternal age' or pregnancy after loss to reduce isolation.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’m doing this whole thing as a solo project, and I think that’s exactly how I’ve been conditioned to handle trauma.

expectations.text: I was hoping for a partner—someone who would acknowledge the weight of my history without making me feel like a liability.
I genuinely thought I could manage the emotions the same way I manage my patients' nutritional intake: assess, plan, intervene, re-evaluate.

perceptions.text: They don't see the woman who lost a baby before and is trying so hard not to let that shadow swallow her whole.
The ultrasound technician... she didn't talk about my age or the risks or the protocols. She just acknowledged that for me, right then, that sound was everything.
I leave every single appointment feeling like I’ve been erased. They provide the medical care, yes, but they completely miss the human being.

valued_items.text: I just needed someone to say, 'I know you're scared, and your history makes this hard. It’s okay to be frightened, but the heartbeat is strong.'
I trust the raw honesty of those women because they aren't trying to manage my 'risk.' They’re just sharing their survival.

missing_items.text: I’ve never had a provider ask me, 'How are you carrying this emotionally?'
I feel so isolated here in Lowell... getting to the clinic on the bus feels like an Olympic event.

service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implementing trauma-informed care protocols that integrate psychosocial screening into every visit.

service_gaps.expectation: Seamless access to care for high-risk, nauseous patients.
service_gaps.perception: Physical, logistical hurdles causing stress-induced physiological spikes.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Telehealth-integrated monitoring or mobile/home-based clinic support for high-risk patients with accessibility barriers.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Getting to the clinic on the bus feels like an Olympic event, especially when I’m this nauseous.

expectations.text: I needed someone to look at me and acknowledge that I knew exactly how thin the ice was beneath us.
I wanted to be a person who was terrified and needed someone to actually listen to my concerns without treating me like a child.

perceptions.text: They’d simplify the explanations, and I’d have to bite my tongue to keep from correcting their terminology.
The hospital gave me the pamphlets... but those papers felt so disconnected from the actual reality of my body.

valued_items.text: I needed to find people who understood that clinical dread isn't something you can just 'meditate' away.
I’m not looking for a silver lining; I’m looking for validation that the trauma I lived through was real.

missing_items.text: The hardest part was realizing that my medical team was done with me the second I was discharged.
I didn't really have anyone who understood the intersection of the medical trauma and the physical healing.

service_gaps.perception: Treated as a child or 'young, single mom' requiring simplification.

service_gaps.expectation: Ongoing support for clinical and emotional aftermath.
service_gaps.perception: Discharged as a 'resolved' case with no follow-up.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Develop a 'Postpartum Transition Plan' that extends care beyond physical recovery into mental health.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: It’s the way a nurse might talk to me like I’m a child who doesn't understand the risks, or the way a doctor ignores my questions because they’ve already decided I’m just 'anxious'.

expectations.text: I wanted someone to look at me, Katelin, and acknowledge that I was drowning in the statistics I knew too well.
I needed someone to sit in the chair next to me and validate the sheer, paralyzing terror of knowing exactly what could have gone wrong.

perceptions.text: They’d walk in and start simplifying things, using that soft, patronizing tone they reserve for young, single moms.
I’m grateful for the medical expertise, obviously—it kept us safe—but the support felt hollow.
It felt like I was being processed through a machine, and once the data points were stable, they figured I was 'fixed.'

valued_items.text: I needed a partner in this, not a gatekeeper.
Seeing that I wasn't the only one dealing with postpartum pre-eclampsia helped quiet that 'I'm the only one' panic.

missing_items.text: I’m still waiting for someone to ask how I’m actually doing.
Nobody acknowledged that maybe, just maybe, my mental health and my physical recovery from a high-risk birth were just as important as the milk itself.

service_gaps.expectation: Expectation of being treated as an intellectual peer/informed patient.
service_gaps.perception: Providers used patronizing, simplified language ignoring her research background.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'patient persona' tagging in medical charts to adapt communication styles.

service_gaps.expectation: Need for holistic, trauma-informed emotional validation.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wanted connection, not a digital clipboard.

expectations.text: I thought knowledge would equal confidence, you know?
I expected the postpartum period to feel more... linear.

perceptions.text: My midwife has been supportive, but sometimes I wonder if my constant tracking and analyzing is actually helping or just feeding my anxiety.
My care was actually really good, clinically speaking... The birth itself was empowering.

valued_items.text: My midwife was fantastic, and I felt empowered by having that continuity of care.
There's something comforting about that collective experience, you know? Like you're not alone in your panic.

missing_items.text: Nobody prepared me for the identity loss... Nobody acknowledged that being smart and knowledgeable doesn't protect you from feeling completely lost.
I wish someone had been more explicit about what 'normal' actually looks like day-to-day... the night sweats, the hormone crashes.

service_gaps.expectation: Knowledge and data-driven preparation lead to confidence and control.
service_gaps.perception: Hyper-fixation on data is a mechanism to manage anxiety, resulting in a loss of connection with the infant.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Move from data-heavy monitoring to mindfulness-based, instinct-building care.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional care includes emotional and identity-based guidance.
service_gaps.perception: Clinical care is excellent, but pivots away when the patient expresses emotional or identity-based distress.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrated 'whole-person' transition support that addresses identity and role shifts.

innovation_opportunities.description: Postpartum 'Identity and Wellness' coaching sessions that go beyond clinical/physical health to address the psychological transition to motherhood.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Not just 'are you bleeding too much?' but 'who are you becoming in this transition?'

innovation_opportunities.description: Guided 'disconnection' or digital detox protocol for new parents to reduce reliance on anxiety-fueling trackers.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: My midwife finally said, 'Elva, put the phone down. Your body knows what to do.'

innovation_opportunities.description: Peer-facilitated support groups focused on the 'messy reality' and grief of life transition, rather than just clinical troubleshooting.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Future care needs to hold space for the messy emotional reality, not just the biological one.

expectations.text: I thought good postnatal care meant efficient, evidence-based monitoring. Regular appointments to check vitals, maybe some bloodwork, clear guidelines about when to call if something seemed off.
I think I expected that once he was born, I'd just... know what to do, you know? Like all that reading and research would suddenly click into place.

perceptions.text: My midwife would come by and yes, she'd check all the clinical boxes. But then she'd sit with me while I was having a meltdown... instead of just giving me reassurance, she'd help me think through what I was actually afraid of.
The apps are particularly insidious because they make me feel like I'm being scientific and proactive, but really I'm just feeding my anxiety.

valued_items.text: My midwife does validate that, thankfully. She'll answer my questions without judgment, but she also gently pushes back sometimes.
What I've actually needed is someone to sit with me in the uncertainty and say, 'This is hard, and you're doing it anyway.'

missing_items.text: I wish someone had challenged my assumptions earlier. Not aggressively, but... gently. Like, during pregnancy, my midwife could have asked me why I was so attached to breastfeeding, what that meant to me beyond the science.
I think it's integrating mental health screening and support into postpartum care as a standard, not an afterthought. Not just at six weeks—at two weeks, three weeks.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected the medical/clinical model to provide a clear path to 'fix' postpartum challenges.
service_gaps.perception: Clinical care is present but leaves the 'emotional' and 'mental' recovery completely unsupported.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrated psychological support/screening milestones before the 6-week checkup.

service_gaps.expectation: Assumption that research and intellect would lead to competence.
service_gaps.perception: Information overload causes anxiety, comparison, and a failure to address the visceral experience of motherhood.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Guidance on 'digital detox' and emotional-focused rather than data-driven postpartum support.

innovation_opportunities.description: Proactive mental health triaging and referral system embedded at the 2-week mark, removing the barrier of the patient needing to self-refer.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I needed the system to do the work for me. I didn't have the capacity to advocate for myself.

innovation_opportunities.description: Prenatal 'expectation auditing' to challenge rigid mental models regarding breastfeeding and identity before the birth occurs.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wish someone had challenged my assumptions earlier... 'You're very knowledgeable about the biology. But what do you actually want for yourself as a mother?'

expectations.text: I think I thought it would just happen instinctively, like our bodies would just know what to do together.
I was supposed to feel this glow of motherhood and instead I feel... untethered.

perceptions.text: The first lactation consultant visit felt like a performance review. She was kind, but I felt like I was being graded.
My midwife was good about home visits. She'd check my incision, ask how I was feeling, but it was always very clinical.

valued_items.text: My midwife was great — attentive, non-judgmental, thorough. I felt supported through that [pregnancy].
There were other women my age talking about similar things — the anxiety, the physical recovery, the identity crisis. That felt good. Less alone.

missing_items.text: And the emotional piece of recovery? Completely absent from the conversations.
Nobody asked me what I needed. What would make me feel capable as a mother?

service_gaps.expectation: Expecting breastfeeding to be an instinctive, natural process.
service_gaps.perception: Feeling judged and incompetent during lactation consultations.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Move from clinical 'grading' to empathy-based, autonomy-focused feeding support.

service_gaps.expectation: Expecting to feel the 'glow' of motherhood.
service_gaps.perception: Experiencing profound identity loss, anxiety, and grief.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement routine, validated, open-ended mental health screenings that allow for honest disclosure.

innovation_opportunities.description: Postpartum 'Identity & Wellness' check-ins that move beyond infant-only health metrics.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want the care system to build in real mental health support... someone who validates that it's okay to grieve your old life while loving your new baby.

innovation_opportunities.description: Digital peer-support moderation tools to prevent 'echo-chamber' anxiety loops.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: The internet gives me information and anxiety in equal measure.

innovation_opportunities.description: Shared decision-making tools for feeding that explicitly include maternal mental health as a variable.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Would supplementing with formula while I figured out breastfeeding make me feel less anxious? Probably.

expectations.text: I thought I'd feel more... instinctual?
I always assumed I'd breastfeed... like it's supposed to just happen.

perceptions.text: My midwife has been wonderful about the clinical stuff.
I can feel her assessment. Not judgment exactly, but like she's checking boxes.

valued_items.text: He just... sat next to me. He took the baby... but then he brought me water and just sat there. Didn't say much.

missing_items.text: What I really needed was someone to say, 'This is hard and you're not broken.'
I need people to check in on me—the actual me—not just the postpartum body or the breastfeeding problem.

service_gaps.expectation: Midwifery support for the whole person during transition.
service_gaps.perception: Clinical checklist-based monitoring of physical recovery only.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Redesign postnatal visits to include holistic, non-clinical wellbeing assessments.

service_gaps.expectation: Seamless breastfeeding success as an instinctual act.
service_gaps.perception: Fragmented, contradictory advice leading to feelings of inadequacy.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Centralized, validated digital hub for breastfeeding support that validates emotional grief.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'Postpartum Transition' program that offers regular, scheduled non-clinical mental health check-ins before crises occur.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Someone whose job was to catch me before I ended up sobbing on my bed.

innovation_opportunities.description: Integrate 'Identity and Life-Transition' coaching into standard postnatal care to address the intersection of career, partnership, and new motherhood.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Nobody talked about what it actually costs to be split like this—professionally, emotionally, physically.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a 'Partner-Included' postpartum session to facilitate proactive discussions on mental load and shared responsibility.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: We kind of fell into these roles without choosing them... We should have talked about this before she was born.

expectations.text: I want someone to see me as a mother who’s scared, not just a nurse ticking boxes.
Real listening, follow-up that isn’t clinical—home visits, peer support, someone who asks 'how are you really?' and waits for the answer.

perceptions.text: I use the right words, I nod, and they mark me medium risk for mental health—but I don’t always say how lonely rural life feels at 3:00 AM during a panic attack.
I put on my 'professional' face.

valued_items.text: Real listening, follow-up that isn’t clinical—home visits, peer support, someone who asks 'how are you really?'

missing_items.text: someone who asks 'how are you really?' and waits for the answer.
follow-up that isn’t clinical

service_gaps.expectation: To be seen as a scared mother needing emotional support rather than a clinician ticking boxes.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a clinical subject ('mark me medium risk') due to her professional persona.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Redesign postnatal intake forms and encounters to prioritize relational inquiry over clinical checklists, especially for healthcare professionals.

innovation_opportunities.description: Peer-to-peer support network for healthcare professionals navigating the transition to motherhood to mitigate 'professional mask' barriers.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’m a pediatric nurse... I put on my 'professional' face.

innovation_opportunities.description: Non-clinical, home-based 'well-being' check-ins by trained peers or dedicated mental health support staff.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Real listening, follow-up that isn’t clinical—home visits, peer support

expectations.text: I just want to find someone who won't treat me like I'm stupid because I'm young and single, but also won't make me feel like a walking statistics spreadsheet.
I need someone who will respect my intellect but also acknowledge that I'm vulnerable right now.

perceptions.text: I'd go in with a list of things I wanted to discuss... and I'd watch them mentally recalibrate. 'Oh, so you've been reading about this?'
One doctor actually suggested I talk to a therapist about my anxiety levels before getting pregnant... it felt like she was pathologizing my caution.

valued_items.text: I need both — the respect and the actual human support.
I need someone who will respect my intellect but also acknowledge that I'm vulnerable right now.

missing_items.text: I haven't really found that balance yet.
The thing nobody tells you is that medical knowledge doesn't actually make you feel safer. It just gives you more specific things to be afraid of.

service_gaps.expectation: Collaborative, intellectually respectful partnership regarding screening/testing options.
service_gaps.perception: Providers dismiss or pathologize her curiosity and medical knowledge as 'difficult' or 'neurotic'.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Create a 'Patient Profile' tool that allows patients to indicate their preferred communication style and knowledge level prior to consult.

innovation_opportunities.description: Preconception consultation pathway specifically designed for high-health-literacy patients who need emotional bridging.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I'm trying to bridge this impossible gap between the clinical observer and the actual human experiencing it.

innovation_opportunities.description: Provider-training module on managing 'informed patients' without bias, particularly addressing age and marital status stigma.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I'm scared that my background is going to make me a difficult patient. That providers will see my questions and think I'm challenging them.

expectations.text: I want someone to treat me like I'm both competent and scared.
I need someone who will take me seriously as both a person with legitimate medical knowledge and as someone who is genuinely scared.

perceptions.text: The conversation lasted maybe three minutes. She asked if I was sexually active, I said no, and she basically said, 'Well, call us back when that changes.'
I get treated like I'm a teenager who just needs basic sex ed.

valued_items.text: I want to be a mother who understands her kid on a deep level — not just the emotional stuff, but the science of development.
I'm disciplined, I'm stable financially... and I have support from my family.

missing_items.text: I wish I didn't have to prepare in secret, like it's something shameful.
Didn't explore my intentions or my family history or anything that would actually be relevant.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional, clinically sophisticated discussion regarding family planning.
service_gaps.perception: Dismissive, age-biased interaction focused on basic sex-ed.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Provider training on non-judgmental, patient-centered inquiry for young, highly informed patients.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I can't seem to communicate that I'm looking for something more sophisticated without sounding defensive.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a peer-support or specialized clinical consult track for 'non-traditional' family planners (e.g., solo, young) to validate their plans and reduce isolation.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: The hardest feeling to acknowledge is loneliness. I think about this constantly, and I can't talk to anyone about it in a real way.

expectations.text: I thought I'd be further along in processing it by now

I imagine being able to advocate for my kid in medical settings because I speak the language. I thought that would be my superpower.

perceptions.text: The doctors here are fine. They're competent. But it's exhausting being treated like I'm just a 'case' sometimes instead of... a person

valued_items.text: I think what I needed, especially after my loss, was someone to actually see me.
I wish someone had asked if I was actually okay, you know?

missing_items.text: The hardest part was that nobody asked about my actual life situation. Like, they didn't say, 'How are you getting here? Do you have support at home?'
I think what I'm missing is someone who's actually in my life, you know? Someone I could call at 2 AM when I'm freaking out.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I think what I'm missing is someone who's actually in my life, you know? Someone I could call at 2 AM when I'm freaking out.

expectations.text: I want someone to actually ask me how I'm doing emotionally, you know? Not just about my labs.

perceptions.text: The doctors are competent. But I feel like they see "23-year-old, previous loss, medium risk" and they just check boxes.

valued_items.text: I appreciate you saying that about no right or wrong answers. Honestly, that's kind of rare to hear from someone in healthcare.

missing_items.text: I don't have family support here. No partner. My friends are scattered... there's no one to just hold my hand and say it'll be okay.

expectations.text: I imagined it would be… a partnership. I wanted someone who would actually look at me, not just the chart.

perceptions.text: The staff, they’re efficient, I’ll give them that. They tick all their boxes and keep my chart in order, but it’s all so clinical.

They just made me feel like I was being 'difficult' or 'anxious.'

valued_items.text: I wish just one of them would look me in the eye and ask, 'Are you okay, Brandi?'
I wanted a teammate, but I got a technician.

missing_items.text: None of them seem to notice that I’m vibrating with anxiety, or that my hands are shaking when they check my vitals.

missing_items.text: I needed a referral to a counselor who understood pregnancy trauma, or even just a warm hand on my shoulder.

valued_items.text: They’re getting stronger, and that’s the one time I don’t feel like just a data point in a chart. It’s like a little secret conversation between the two of us.

missing_items.text: I just wish someone had sat with me and asked, 'How are you feeling about all this?' instead of just waiting for me to sign the forms.

perceptions.text: Everything about the clinical side is fine, I guess. My midwife is kind, but she’s always checking her watch.

valued_items.text: She didn't rush me to get dressed. She just let me sit there for a second and hold that moment.

missing_items.text: If someone just sat down, looked at me, and asked, 'How is the rest of your life going? Who is going to be there when you get home today?'

valued_items.text: She explained my progesterone levels in a way that made sense... It makes me feel less like a passive body and more like... a partner in this process.
Sarah has been my main real-world support, honestly. She's the only person who sees me regularly and knows the whole picture.

perceptions.text: The midwife I see—she's actually done pretty well with that. She listens when I ask about progesterone levels or why they're not doing certain screenings. She doesn't talk down to me, and that matters.
There's this underlying thing I feel sometimes, like she's being extra careful with me because of my age or my marital status. Like she's worried I might not follow through or something.

valued_items.text: When she tells me why they're not doing certain interventions, or why they want to monitor something—that matters to me. She doesn't just say 'we do it this way,' she says 'here's what the evidence shows.'

missing_items.text: I also wanted someone to ask me how I'm doing with all of this. Not the baby—me. Like, how am I handling being alone in this?

valued_items.text: There's something about the anonymity [of forums] that lets me be honest.
