expectations.text: I knew I wanted to breastfeed. That was the plan from the beginning—I'd seen the benefits, read all the research.
I'm supposed to be enjoying this, right? I'm supposed to feel like a natural, like this is the most beautiful time of my life.

perceptions.text: My OB was great, don't get me wrong, but after delivery, it was like a light switch. Suddenly I was just... handed my baby and sent home.
The hospital lactation consultant came in, and she was helpful enough, but I felt like she was rushing.
My six-week checkup was literally five minutes long. Nobody asked how I was actually doing.

valued_items.text: There's this private Facebook group for nurses with kids. That one's been... actually kind of validating?

missing_items.text: Nobody asked me about that part [the emotional struggle]. They just kept checking my vitals and asking if I was eating enough.
I wish someone had sat down with me and said, 'What matters most to you, Chasity?' instead of assuming they already knew.

service_gaps.expectation: Personalized support and guidance regarding emotional well-being.
service_gaps.perception: Service providers assumed expertise due to professional background and ignored emotional check-ins.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement systematic, non-judgmental postpartum mental health screenings that bypass clinical expertise assumptions.

service_gaps.expectation: Comprehensive transition support post-delivery.
service_gaps.perception: Care stopped abruptly as a 'light switch' after discharge.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Create a structured, multi-week postpartum navigation program beyond the 6-week clinical checkup.

innovation_opportunities.description: Healthcare-provider-specific peer support groups to normalize the 'curse of knowing' and reduce professional isolation.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Someone posted about obsessively checking her baby's breathing at night, and like fifty nurses were like, 'Yeah, me too. It's the curse of knowing too much.'

innovation_opportunities.description: Digital 'Whole-Person' check-in tool that allows patients to report anxiety/mental health concerns privately to clinicians.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I haven't talked to my OB about any of this... I think I'm using these groups as a substitute for actual medical help.

innovation_opportunities.description: Standardized 'Value-Based' care planning consultations that prioritize personal preferences over clinical defaults.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wish someone had sat down with me and said, 'What matters most to you, Chasity?' instead of assuming they already knew.

expectations.text: I figured I had the clinical side down.
I thought I'd feel this immediate bonding, this instinctive joy.
I expected the physical recovery to be manageable.

perceptions.text: The hospital team discharged me like I was just another low-risk case... they didn't ask about my support system.
I left that appointment feeling more invisible than ever.
She was kind—genuinely kind—but I couldn't accept that kindness because it meant admitting I didn't have it figured out.

valued_items.text: I needed someone to treat me like a vulnerable person, not a competent professional.
I needed someone to say it was okay to struggle. To name that the gap between knowing and doing is real.

missing_items.text: Nobody asked about my mood. Didn't ask if I was struggling with intrusive thoughts... Didn't ask if being back at work while pumping was actually sustainable.
Nobody said, 'Hey, you're a single mom in a small town. Do you have people?'

service_gaps.expectation: Professional competence would translate to an easy transition to motherhood.
service_gaps.perception: Professional knowledge creates a 'curse' of anxiety and inability to seek help.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Peer support networks for healthcare professionals who are new parents to dismantle the 'must be perfect' culture.

service_gaps.expectation: Clinical discharge process covers 'preparedness' for home.
service_gaps.perception: Discharge felt routine and completely lacked social or emotional support planning.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implementation of a holistic, non-clinical discharge 'preparedness checklist' that assesses social support and mental wellbeing.

innovation_opportunities.description: Provider training to specifically identify and de-stigmatize mental health struggles in 'high-functioning' or healthcare-worker patients.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: The biggest gap was that I needed someone to treat me like a vulnerable person, not a competent professional.

innovation_opportunities.description: A 'Supportive Transition' tool that connects single mothers or those without immediate support to local resources before discharge.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Nobody said, 'Hey, you're a single mom in a small town. Do you have people?' They just... assumed I'd be fine.

innovation_opportunities.description: Digital check-in platform that prompts for mental health and support-system status between appointments, bypassing the fear of face-to-face judgment.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I was looking for a way out that didn't involve talking to anyone. Because talking to someone means I have to say the words out loud.

expectations.text: I thought I'd be this calm, informed person sailing through it all.
I had this almost romanticized vision of it being this natural, beautiful thing.

perceptions.text: My pediatrician keeps treating me like we're peers—she uses clinical terms... and I just... I wanted someone to see that I'm scared.
The lactation consultant... helped technically, but I remember thinking: I should already know this.
The apps fed directly into my anxiety. Every time I logged a feed, the app would generate these charts and analytics, and I'd spiral.

valued_items.text: My husband finally got through to me... 'You're not a nutrition specialist right now. You're a mom who's terrified.'
I found a therapist who specializes in postpartum anxiety and trauma.

missing_items.text: Nobody connected the dots between my anxiety, my trauma, my physical recovery, and my feeding struggles.
I'd want integrated mental health screening that actually digs into trauma history, not just yes-or-no depression questions.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional care that addresses the whole person.
service_gaps.perception: Fragmented, siloed, and overly clinical care.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrated care navigation platform

service_gaps.expectation: Support for emotional well-being during physical recovery.
service_gaps.perception: Dismissive clinical checks focusing only on medical 'normalcy'.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Trauma-informed screening tools for postpartum visits

innovation_opportunities.description: Trauma-informed postpartum check-in protocols that screen for historical triggers, not just standard postpartum depression.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I'd want integrated mental health screening that actually digs into trauma history, not just yes-or-no depression questions.

innovation_opportunities.description: Cross-disciplinary care coordination role (Anchor) to bridge the gap between Pediatrics, OB, and Mental Health.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I'd want one person to be my anchor. Not passing me between specialists, but actually coordinating.

innovation_opportunities.description: Redesign of digital tracking apps to allow for 'data-free' modes or focus on qualitative wellbeing instead of quantitative metrics.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I had the feeding tracker... It became this data obsession that was just... unhealthy.

expectations.text: I came into this thinking my knowledge would be protective somehow... that I could control the outcome
I hoped someone would ask me how I was actually doing. Not 'How are you feeling?' in that perfunctory way

perceptions.text: The pediatrician actually seems relieved when she talks to me because she doesn't have to explain things. But that's made me feel almost invisible
She kept reassuring me it was normal... but her reassurance felt like pity... like she was talking down to the panicked new mother I'd become
She said... 'Well, you're a nutrition specialist, so I'm sure you're managing the feeding piece beautifully.' That was it. Ten minutes.

valued_items.text: What I really needed postpartum wasn't someone who could discuss lactoferrin with me. I needed someone to see that I was scared.

missing_items.text: I needed someone to ask about my mental health directly, not assume I had it handled because I'm competent in other areas.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional knowledge as a tool for safety and control
service_gaps.perception: Professional knowledge used by providers as a justification for withholding support
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Provider bias training regarding high-achieving/educated patients

service_gaps.expectation: Meaningful, holistic check-in at 6 weeks
service_gaps.perception: Perfunctory, logistically focused appointment
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Standardized, validated screening tools for mental health that bypass provider assumption

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop 'High-Functioning Patient' protocols for maternity care that explicitly mandate mental health screening regardless of the patient's professional background.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: don't let someone's knowledge fool you into thinking they're fine. Sometimes the most dangerous patients are the ones who understand exactly what's going wrong.

innovation_opportunities.description: Peer support networks specifically for 'expert' mothers who feel isolated by their professional knowledge base.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I was looking for people who were struggling despite having all the knowledge, because that would prove I wasn't uniquely broken.

innovation_opportunities.description: Structured, non-clinical 'debrief' sessions at the 6-week postnatal check, distinct from the physical exam, focusing on identity and mental wellbeing.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I hoped someone would ask me how I was actually doing. Not 'How are you feeling?' in that perfunctory way.

expectations.text: I wanted a conversation about what support looks like when you don't have a village, or how to handle the inevitable triggers that come with a crying infant
I thought there would be a nurse practitioner or a midwife who would ask, 'Gloria, how are you actually doing?'

perceptions.text: The appointment lasted ten minutes... she didn't look at me. She didn’t see the way my hands shake
It felt like I was being prepared for a standard, factory-line experience

valued_items.text: what I wanted more than anything was to be seen. Not as a 'low-risk patient' or a young woman with a certain demographic profile, but as a person with a history
someone who sits with me when I talk about my fear of repeating the past

missing_items.text: I feel like I’m doing it in a vacuum, without any real guidance on how to hold the weight of all this emotional preparation
I’m looking for a provider who understands that a healthy pregnancy isn’t just about folic acid and blood pressure—it’s about preparing the soil

service_gaps.expectation: Seeking a partnership to navigate trauma and mental health preparation.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a low-risk physical metrics case with no interest in emotional history.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: High - Requires integration of mental health screening and trauma-informed care at the preconception level.

innovation_opportunities.description: Trauma-Informed Preconception Consultations: A dedicated, longer-duration appointment focused on psychosocial history and emotional regulation tools rather than just physical metrics.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: If the medical system could just carve out five minutes to look past the blood pressure and the age, to actually look at the person sitting in front of them

innovation_opportunities.description: Digital Peer-Support & Resilience Toolkits: A curated resource platform for women without a village to learn nervous system regulation and attachment-based parenting.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want to offer a child a version of me that is steady... I’m looking for a path that feels like it’s being erased as soon as you find it.

expectations.text: I was hoping for some kind of validation—not just that my body was ready, but that I was ready. I wanted her to say, 'I see your history, and here is how we’re going to hold space for you.'
I’m looking for someone to see me, not just a chart.

perceptions.text: The clinical environment is so cold... She gave me a brochure about folic acid, but there wasn't a single line in it about how to survive the fear of yourself.
It felt like she was talking to a machine, not a person.

valued_items.text: I wanted a human connection, someone who could look at me and acknowledge that I’m trying to survive my past while planning for a future. I didn't want a pamphlet; I wanted a witness.
The only things that feel even remotely 'credible' are the forums or blogs where other women are actually talking... it feels like a mirror.

missing_items.text: I’m looking for a roadmap to resilience, but the internet only ever gives me a list of reasons to be afraid.
There’s no professional bridge, no one to say, 'Yes, this is scary, but here is how we handle it.'

service_gaps.expectation: Seeking a provider to acknowledge history and hold space for psychological/trauma needs.
service_gaps.perception: Doctor focused strictly on physiological markers (blood pressure/vitals) and provided generic handouts.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement trauma-informed care intake protocols that prioritize patient psychological history alongside clinical vitals.

innovation_opportunities.description: Trauma-informed care pathways that integrate mental health screening and 'witnessing' into standard preconception visits.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want a provider who knows that the most important thing they can offer me isn't a prescription—it's a little bit of grace and the patience to listen.

innovation_opportunities.description: A 'Resilience Roadmap' tool that bridges the gap between clinical data and the lived experience of pregnancy-related anxiety.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need someone who can translate the science into something that doesn't feel like a death sentence for my peace of mind.

expectations.text: I think I expected it would be like before. Quick and easy.
I felt like we could be partners in figuring this out together.

perceptions.text: She just told me to keep trying, like I'm not already doing that every single day... she just smiled at me. That smile. Like I was being cute or paranoid.
My doctor keeps saying I'm 'low risk' like that's supposed to be comforting, but that's not — that's not the point.

valued_items.text: I just want my doctor to actually hear me... Not as a low-risk statistic, but as a person who knows her own body.
I'm being proactive about my own health, and that should be seen as a strength.

missing_items.text: If I ever needed to see a specialist or get real help, it's not like I can just pop into the city.
She didn't ask me how long we'd been trying, didn't take my concerns seriously, didn't offer any real plan.

service_gaps.expectation: Partnering with a professional to investigate fertility concerns.
service_gaps.perception: Being dismissed as 'low risk' and paranoid by a GP.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement structured clinical triage tools for pre-conception consultations.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional guidance on health and fertility tracking.
service_gaps.perception: Forced to rely solely on internet research and forums without medical validation.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Digital health platforms for verified, doctor-approved preconception education.

innovation_opportunities.description: Telehealth-integrated fertility tracking dashboard that allows patients to share their self-gathered data directly with clinicians before appointments.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I've read about it, and I just wanted to explore options... I brought notes, I had questions about my cycle.

innovation_opportunities.description: Virtual specialist consultation pathways for rural patients to overcome geographic isolation.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: If I ever needed to see a specialist... it's not like I can just pop into the city. That stress alone.

expectations.text: I'm looking for someone who takes me seriously.
I'm hoping she'll actually see me this time... look at my specific numbers, my cycle patterns, and say, 'Okay, Corie, here's what I'm noticing.'

perceptions.text: She glanced at my charts for maybe two seconds and said, 'Well, you have three healthy pregnancies already, so clearly your body works fine.'
She gave me this smile... the patronizing smile... and said, 'You're overthinking this. Just relax and keep trying.'

valued_items.text: Your concerns are valid. Your body isn't broken. Let's figure this out together.
I'd trust an app more if it was backed by actual research... developed by fertility specialists.

missing_items.text: That gap between information and actual help — that's what keeps me anxious.
It's not just an appointment, it's this whole day trip... if I actually need real help — like a specialist or something — it feels impossible.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional clinical review of personalized cycle data and symptoms.
service_gaps.perception: Dismissal of data as paranoia/overthinking with no examination.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Digital health passport integration for patients to submit verified data to clinicians prior to visits.

service_gaps.expectation: Local access to specialist fertility support.
service_gaps.perception: Impossible travel logistics making specialized care feel inaccessible.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Tele-fertility consultation pathways for rural patients to reduce travel burden.

innovation_opportunities.description: Pre-consultation digital portal for data sharing, allowing patients to upload charts/logs that doctors are required to acknowledge as part of the clinical intake.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I brought my charts with me... She looked at my charts for maybe thirty seconds.

innovation_opportunities.description: Provider communication training modules focused on validating patient anxiety to reduce dismissive interactions.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Not dismissal. Never that again.

innovation_opportunities.description: Hybrid care model combining local GP oversight with virtual specialist consultations to overcome geographical barriers.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Living out here, if I actually need real help — like a specialist or something — it feels impossible.

expectations.text: I should feel seasoned

perceptions.text: Sometimes I feel like a chart, not a person
the providers are professional

valued_items.text: I leave wanting someone to ask how I’ll get food on the table or who’ll watch the kids

missing_items.text: I leave wanting someone to ask how I’ll get food on the table or who’ll watch the kids if I’m admitted.
I haven’t been able to afford to fix [wisdom tooth]

service_gaps.expectation: Personalized, holistic support for a high-risk pregnancy.
service_gaps.perception: Transactional, clinical interaction focused solely on biometric markers ('I feel like a chart').
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integration of social determinants of health (SDOH) screening and navigation into standard antenatal visits.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a Holistic Social Needs Screening tool during antenatal intake to connect high-risk patients with community resources (food, childcare, dental).
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I leave wanting someone to ask how I’ll get food on the table or who’ll watch the kids if I’m admitted.

innovation_opportunities.description: Introduce a 'Person-Centered Care' bridge tool that allows patients to document their social/family stressors alongside medical charts to improve provider empathy.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Sometimes I feel like a chart, not a person

expectations.text: I want a provider who listens, who explains choices like a partner, not like a conveyor belt.

perceptions.text: It feels like someone else is steering. Being labeled high risk has made every little thing feel urgent and out of my hands.
This is the third different midwife you've spoken to this pregnancy... those small choices feel overwhelmed by the system.

valued_items.text: having one real voice to answer me helps more than scrolling endless horror tales.
ask for clear explanations, or say I want time to think before signing anything.

missing_items.text: I want a provider who listens, who explains choices like a partner, not like a conveyor belt.
Practical support (logistics for childcare/finances) integrated into high-risk clinical advice.

service_gaps.expectation: Partner-style collaborative decision-making.
service_gaps.perception: Feeling that medical urgency (high-risk status) overrides personal choice and agency.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'Decision Aid' tools that allow patients to document their preferences alongside medical advice before appointments.

service_gaps.expectation: Consistent, personal relationship with a care provider.
service_gaps.perception: Fragmented care from multiple, unfamiliar midwives.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Digital patient portal showing a 'Core Care Team' profile to provide familiarity even when rotating staff.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'Clinical Navigation' service that addresses the intersection of clinical care and practical barriers (childcare, employment, finance) for high-risk patients.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: When they talk about hospital stays or induction, my first thought is logistics: who watches the kids, who pays the bills, will I lose my job?

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a vetted, clinician-moderated messaging or Q&A platform to provide accurate, calming information to replace the anxiety-inducing 'pregnancy forum' loop.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Sometimes I force myself to find one calm story, or I call the midwife’s office in the morning to ask a question — something small, like how to count movements properly.

expectations.text: I had this fantasy that being in healthcare would make it easier. That I'd find providers who got me, who I could have real conversations with about the clinical details.
I wanted to be treated like a patient, not a colleague playing the role of patient.

perceptions.text: My OB is competent... She's thorough, efficient, and we speak the same language professionally. She knows I work in healthcare, so there's this shorthand between us.
I think that same efficiency sometimes feels like she's checking boxes rather than actually seeing me.
There was no conversation about what these tests mean for me, about my values, about what I'd do with different results.

valued_items.text: The ultrasound tech was kind, at least. She let me look at the screen for longer, explained what we were seeing.

missing_items.text: I wish someone would ask me how I'm really doing. Not the clinical version — the actual version.
What I actually needed and didn't ask for? Someone I could talk to who understands both the science and the fear.

service_gaps.expectation: Meaningful clinical conversation and peer-level engagement.
service_gaps.perception: Transactional, 'check-box' efficiency.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement structured psychosocial check-ins that bypass the 'colleague' persona.

service_gaps.expectation: Support in navigating personal values and decision-making.
service_gaps.perception: Pamphlet-based, non-interactive information delivery.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Value-based decision aid tools that explicitly address emotional context alongside clinical risks.

innovation_opportunities.description: Provider prompt-tool or 'emotional triage' protocol to break through the 'colleague-patient' barrier.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I think what I needed — what I still need — is for someone to slow down and ask, 'What are you actually worried about? What matters to you in how we approach this?'

innovation_opportunities.description: Dedicated emotional/clinical hybrid support service (e.g., specialized counseling) that validates the specific anxiety of healthcare professionals in pregnancy.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Someone who wouldn't dismiss my concerns because they're technically unfounded, but also wouldn't feed into the anxiety spiral.

expectations.text: I want to trust the process. I want to trust my body.
I think I need permission to be a person first and a nurse second.

perceptions.text: My OB is wonderful — we understand each other professionally, and she doesn't treat me like I'm fragile.
I see my OB regularly, but for routine stuff, it's whoever's available. So every single time I call with a question or get a result, I'm starting from scratch.
I feel like I have to hide who I am to get good care. That being educated somehow disqualifies me from being vulnerable.

valued_items.text: She wasn't overselling the tests or being alarmist. She just presented the data and asked what I wanted to do.

missing_items.text: I wish someone had asked me what I actually wanted, not just what the data suggested.
I need someone to see me enough to know that I'm drowning... I need therapy before the baby comes.

service_gaps.expectation: Trusting the process and body through professional guidance
service_gaps.perception: Performing competence to avoid being labeled as an 'anxious' patient
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Create a 'peer-specialist' or psychological support track for healthcare professionals in maternity care to separate clinical knowledge from emotional burden.

service_gaps.expectation: Consistent, personalized care interaction
service_gaps.perception: Transactional, impersonal interactions with rotating staff who do not know history
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement a 'Care Concierge' or 'Patient Biography' digital tool that flags specific emotional or history-based context for every staff member who touches the case.

innovation_opportunities.description: Provider-to-patient 'emotional health' screening that looks beyond clinical markers to identify 'professional patients' masking distress.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: No one's screening me for this stuff because I don't look like someone who's struggling. My blood pressure's fine... But I'm drowning

innovation_opportunities.description: Integration of 'Vulnerability Briefings' where patients can opt-in to express fears that sit outside of clinical risk assessments.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I think I need permission to be a person first and a nurse second. But I don't know how to ask for that.

expectations.text: I hoped for a doctor or midwife who would sit down and say, 'Catarina, tell me about your life. Tell me what you're worried about. Tell me how you're really doing.'
I think if I saw the same midwife or doctor every time, maybe it would be different. Maybe they would remember that I don't understand medical words.

perceptions.text: The clinic is clean, and the staff is nice enough, but I feel like... I'm not really a person to them. I'm a number on a chart.
The appointments... they happen, you know? I go, I wait a long time in the waiting room, I see the doctor or nurse for maybe fifteen minutes, and then I leave. It's very quick.

valued_items.text: But when I felt her move... that changed things. Made it real in a different way. Made me feel less alone.
My mother... she said, 'This is your baby. Your baby is innocent. We will figure this out as a family.'

missing_items.text: Nobody offered to help me understand things better because I was alone, or asked if I needed extra support.
I wish they would explain things slower. Or maybe give me papers in... simpler words. Or even in my language, you know?

service_gaps.expectation: Continuity of provider to build trust and understanding of personal circumstances.
service_gaps.perception: Seeing different providers every time; having to retell the same difficult story repeatedly.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement a primary midwife/physician pairing system for high-risk or vulnerable patient profiles.

service_gaps.expectation: Clear, accessible communication regarding medical procedures and terminology.
service_gaps.perception: High-speed, jargon-heavy delivery that leads to feeling 'lost' and 'guessing' decisions.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Digital patient portal providing visual/translated summaries of diagnostic test results.

innovation_opportunities.description: Multilingual visual aids for common pregnancy screenings and anatomy scans to bridge the language barrier.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wish they would explain things slower. Or maybe give me papers in... simpler words. Or even in my language, you know?

innovation_opportunities.description: Peer support group matching for pregnant immigrants to share cultural/linguistic advice and reduce isolation.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: There's a Facebook group too—for immigrants in Boston. I joined it because I thought maybe other women there would understand the language barrier.

innovation_opportunities.description: Pre-appointment 'Patient Briefing' tool that allows patients to record concerns or language needs before arrival.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: The doctors and nurses, they don't really ask about him anymore... Nobody offered to help me understand things better.

expectations.text: Back home, my mother gave birth with midwives, with her mother there... [They] took time. He talked to you like you were a person, not just... a checkbox on a list.
I was hoping someone had sat down with me—really sat down—and explained what this test actually means.

perceptions.text: Everything with the doctors is very fast, and so many words I don't always understand... I just smile and nod.
Every time I go to the clinic, it's a different person. I don't know their names. They don't know me... I feel like I'm a checkbox sometimes.

valued_items.text: It's nice when someone wants to listen, you know?
My body, it's doing what it's supposed to do. That part... I trust my body.

missing_items.text: Nobody explains things in a connected way... nobody says, 'Okay Catarina, here's the big picture of what happens next.'
I feel like I'm just... going through a machine... who have support systems. But me? I'm just trying not to disappear in the process.

service_gaps.expectation: Expectation of being treated as a person with time for questions and deep understanding.
service_gaps.perception: Treated like a 'checkbox' with fast, script-read interactions that leave no room for uncertainty.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Standardized 'person-centered' handovers that include social/personal history alongside clinical data.

service_gaps.expectation: Continuity with a familiar caregiver who knows the patient's history and personal struggles.
service_gaps.perception: Interaction with a rotating roster of midwives who do not know the patient's history or social context.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Case-managed care models (primary midwife) or digital patient profile portals that synthesize previous interactions.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a 'Personal Context' digital dashboard that populates for every clinician, highlighting language needs, emotional status, and social support factors.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: The clinical details don't matter if nobody sees me as a person. And that's what gets lost.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Warm Handover' process where outgoing midwives introduce the patient to the next caregiver, summarizing not just clinical data but personal concerns.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Every time I go to the clinic, it's a different person... I have to start all over. I have to prove I'm human.

innovation_opportunities.description: Offer 'Decision Support' sessions led by advocates or trained peer supporters to explain test results and procedures in plain language without time pressure.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wasn't really deciding — I was just... agreeing. There's a difference, I think.

expectations.text: I wanted to be treated like I mattered, but not like I had all the answers. I wanted someone who could explain things thoroughly but also recognize when I needed reassurance instead of data.
I think I had this naive hope that my providers would just... see me as a person first, a professional second.

perceptions.text: My OB is competent. She knows her stuff, and I respect her. But there's this weird dynamic that I don't think either of us has quite figured out yet.
There's this unspoken understanding between us that I'm not supposed to be the anxious patient... because if I do, I'm admitting that I'm not as together as I'm supposed to be.

valued_items.text: It's been the same OB, which I think has been good in some ways. Consistency matters. She knows my history.

missing_items.text: What I needed was someone to sit with me and acknowledge that knowing the statistics doesn't mean you're not terrified.
My OB has never asked me directly about support at home. She assumes I'm managing... maybe she'd check in more, or spend more time reassuring me.

service_gaps.expectation: Recognition of vulnerability despite clinical knowledge.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a clinical peer, leading to information overload rather than reassurance.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Provider-patient communication training specifically for clinicians who are also patients.

service_gaps.expectation: Holistic check-ins on support systems.
service_gaps.perception: Assumed self-sufficiency due to patient profile/competence.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Standardized psychosocial assessment tools that trigger deeper inquiry regardless of perceived patient autonomy.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a 'Patient-Provider Persona Toggle' protocol: clinicians ask at the start of visits, 'Would you like to discuss this as a colleague or as a patient today?'
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need her to say 'your baby's heartbeat is strong' without me having to decode her clinical neutrality.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement an 'Emotional Check-in' module in patient portals for high-knowledge patients to express anxiety without needing to verbally admit 'weakness' in person.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I don't bring up the midnight spirals... because if I do, I'm admitting that I'm not as together as I'm supposed to be.

expectations.text: I need her to let me be the patient, not the colleague.
I wanted her to say 'everything is perfect' in a way that felt final, not just measured.
What I actually needed was to hear her tell me why she thought I should do it. Not the data—the recommendation.

perceptions.text: She was treating it like I didn't need the explanation... like I could just make a clinical decision.
They're reading from the chart, not understanding the context of who you are.
My OB—I respect her enormously... I appreciate that collaborative approach. But emotionally? Sometimes I just need her to tell me.

valued_items.text: someone... just saying, 'This sucks, but you're going to be okay.'
I think my care would be completely different if I could just... admit that... I'm afraid, and I need my doctor to know that about me.

missing_items.text: I'm doing this alone... the silence in the car driving home from appointments is real. There's no one to turn to.
What I need isn't more information or more autonomy. It's someone seeing me... and saying, 'You're going to be okay.'

service_gaps.expectation: Needs the doctor to lead and offer a human recommendation.
service_gaps.perception: Doctor defers to the patient's expertise, creating a lack of authoritative reassurance.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Provider training on 'Expert Patient' personas: learning to balance clinical collaboration with emotional guidance.

service_gaps.expectation: Consistent, personal care that understands the patient's context.
service_gaps.perception: Fragmented care across different midwives who only see the 'chart'.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'context-rich' patient profiles in EHRs that highlight psychosocial context beyond clinical history.

innovation_opportunities.description: Provider-led 'Humanization' tool for EHRs that highlights patient-specific psychosocial stressors for new clinical staff during handovers.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: The personal stuff. Absolutely the personal stuff... what gets lost in every handover is the context of who I am.

innovation_opportunities.description: Virtual 'Expert Patient' peer support group, moderated by a facilitator who validates fears without reverting to clinical data/statistics.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: What would actually help... is someone seeing me... and saying, 'You're going to be okay.'

expectations.text: I imagined a relationship with my providers that felt like the ones I used to talk about in my classes: collaborative, calm, and human.
I wanted continuity too, so the person who knew my story would also be the one guiding me through hard decisions.

perceptions.text: my team treats me like a peer and fires off clinical shorthand, and while I appreciate honesty, it often feels cold.
Being unmarried and labeled high-risk made me fear judgment and being bounced between clinicians instead of actually seen.

valued_items.text: what really mattered was the small human stuff nobody measured: someone who sees me as a mother, not a checklist.
I still want a hand on my arm when the cuff goes on.

missing_items.text: who will monitor me for postpartum pre‑eclampsia, who will help with breastfeeding, who will listen when I’m scared.
Kindness matters. Competence without that warmth has made this pregnancy lonelier than I expected.

service_gaps.expectation: Collaborative, calm, and human interaction.
service_gaps.perception: Clinical shorthand and cold, transactional interactions.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Communication training focused on balancing clinical precision with bedside manner.

service_gaps.expectation: Continuity of care with one provider.
service_gaps.perception: Being bounced between different clinicians.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implementation of a 'Named Midwife' or primary lead professional model for high-risk patients.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a postnatal home-visiting program specifically for high-risk patients to bridge the gap between discharge and routine check-ups.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: If systems could offer continuity, time to actually talk, home checks, and someone who says 'you’re safe' instead of reciting risks, it would mean everything.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a 'Patient-Led Narrative' digital tool that sits alongside clinical charts, allowing providers to see the 'mother' behind the 'high-risk label'.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: someone who sees me as a mother, not a checklist.

expectations.text: wanting someone to hold my hand and say, “We’ve got you,” instead of rattling off probabilities.
Continuity — one or two providers who know me and can translate the medical stuff into human words.

perceptions.text: every appointment feels like triage, that I’ve spoken to more new faces than familiar ones
my town-to-clinic drive stretches the worry thin.
rattling off probabilities

valued_items.text: Continuity — one or two providers who know me and can translate the medical stuff into human words.
being allowed to be a mother instead of a diagnosis.

missing_items.text: A real moment of reassurance, not just clinical updates.

Help planning for postpartum, because high-risk births don’t end at delivery.

service_gaps.expectation: Personalized, continuous care and human connection
service_gaps.perception: Triage-based, fragmented appointments with strangers
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement primary midwifery models to ensure the same 1-2 providers throughout the pregnancy journey.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Clear, fast lines for when things feel off at 2am.

innovation_opportunities.description: Postpartum transition planning integration for high-risk patients
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Help planning for postpartum, because high-risk births don’t end at delivery.

innovation_opportunities.description: Translation service/protocol for medical terminology to human-centric communication
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: translate the medical stuff into human words

expectations.text: I want someone to stop treating me like a clinical case study and just look at me like a woman who is scared out of her mind.
I’m not building a relationship; I’m just hitting a series of milestones with strangers.

perceptions.text: The care itself is technically fine, but it’s emotionally vacant.
She didn't treat my nursing background like a barrier to communication. She treated it like a language we both spoke, but she still gave me the space to be the person who was scared.

valued_items.text: The women there don't tell me to 'breathe and stay calm for the baby.' They tell me, 'I know exactly what you’re feeling, and it’s okay to be scared.'
She didn't look at the screen. She just squeezed, and for that breath, I wasn't 'the high-risk case in bed four.' I was a person.

missing_items.text: I feel like I have to fight to get them to stop treating me like a colleague and start treating me like a patient who needs to be heard.
I don’t have that [a partner to anchor me]. If I have a crisis, it’s going to be me on the phone with the ambulance.

service_gaps.expectation: Desire for human-centered, empathetic care that acknowledges fear.
service_gaps.perception: Providers used clinical shorthand and ignored emotional distress, assuming her medical background made her immune to fear.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Staff sensitivity training on the 'Hidden Patient'—professionals who need emotional buffering.

service_gaps.expectation: Consistent, relationship-based continuity of care.
service_gaps.perception: Rotational staffing forced her to re-explain her complex history repeatedly to strangers.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Designated 'Primary Midwife' or 'Care Navigator' for high-risk, medically literate patients.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a peer-support vetting protocol for high-risk patients that bypasses clinical 'professional courtesy' barriers.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need someone to tell me that I’m not a failure for having it.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Care Preference' flag that allows patients to opt-out of professional/technical communication styles in favor of lay-language.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Because they knew I worked in the unit, they figured I didn't need the 'patient version.'

expectations.text: I just want someone to look at me and tell me it’s going to be okay, without me having to translate the clinical terms myself.
Please use my name, not just 'the patient', and, 'If a decision needs to be made, please explain it in plain English'.

perceptions.text: They treat me like a colleague. They use all this shorthand, assuming I’m 'resilient' because I know the mechanics of it all.
There is no continuity of care, just a revolving door of clinicians who have to skim my chart before they start talking.
The midwife was efficient—professional, sure—but she had another patient waiting... It’s a subtle kind of coercion.

valued_items.text: That document [birth plan] was the only place I felt like I had a shred of agency left.
I need someone to just hold my hand—not to check my pulse, but to ground me.

missing_items.text: The leaflet was full of lists of what I should eat, but it didn't have a single line about how to afford it.
I’m constantly terrified that because there’s no one person tracking my story, the one thing that really matters is going to be missed.

service_gaps.expectation: Needs to be seen as a person/mother, not a professional peer.
service_gaps.perception: Staff rely on her medical background to avoid emotional care and explanation.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Staff training on 'professional patient' bias mitigation.

service_gaps.expectation: Continuity and personal rapport with clinicians.
service_gaps.perception: Revolving door of midwives leads to patient feeling like a 'case file'.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Digital 'patient story' summary dashboard for handover.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: The leaflet was full of lists of what I should eat, but it didn't have a single line about how to afford it.

innovation_opportunities.description: Standardized 'Human Connection' protocol for high-stress obstetric procedures, requiring staff to perform a 10-second 'grounding' intervention (eye contact/touch) before initiating clinical tasks.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They talked around me, not to me... There wasn't a single touch that was meant to comfort.

expectations.text: I need someone who sees me as a person first, not just a pregnant body or a case number.
I want to labor at home as long as possible, in a familiar environment where I feel safer.

perceptions.text: My midwife, she's actually really sweet. She listens.
The system itself feels impersonal too. All the paperwork, the forms asking the same questions over and over. Nobody really asks about the trauma part.
There's a familiarity that helps a little. Like, I don't have to explain myself from scratch every time I walk in.

valued_items.text: It's the same midwife most of the time, which... honestly, that's probably better than the alternative.
She's never made me feel bad about my anxiety.

missing_items.text: I wish there was more consistency with the emotional side of things, though. Like, one appointment she'll check in about how I'm feeling mentally, and the next it's just all clinical.
I wish there was something between Google and a support group. Like, accessible mental health support that actually gets trauma.

service_gaps.expectation: Personalized, trauma-informed care that acknowledges her history.
service_gaps.perception: Systemic reliance on repetitive, impersonal paperwork and check-boxes.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Digital trauma-informed intake histories that transfer between visits to avoid repetitive questioning.

service_gaps.expectation: Emotional and psychological support integrated into clinical visits.
service_gaps.perception: Appointments shift between clinical check-ups and emotional check-ins, feeling fragmented.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrated care pathways where emotional screening results trigger specific support protocols alongside clinical data.

innovation_opportunities.description: Trauma-informed digital pre-appointment briefing tool where patients can flag specific triggers (e.g., doppler, internal exams) for the provider.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Every time she reaches for the doppler or wants to do a cervical check, I feel this cold panic wash over me.

innovation_opportunities.description: Low-barrier 'trauma-aware' professional counseling services that bridge the gap between self-led research and intensive group therapy.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wish there was something between Google and a support group. Like, accessible mental health support that actually gets trauma.

expectations.text: I thought it would feel more familiar, more normal.
If there had been one consistent person checking in on me... I think I would've felt safer.

perceptions.text: The rotating midwives thing... having a stranger tell you something about your pregnancy feels... impersonal.
I'm just a case number, not a person.
My midwife is kind, really she is.

valued_items.text: I appreciate that you're actually... listening.
He'll say, 'Can you explain what you're looking at?' when I'm just sitting there silent.

missing_items.text: I wish my midwife had asked me more about why I wanted it... I'm not sure I had real autonomy.
Nobody asked what I needed... They just assumed the baby was enough.

service_gaps.expectation: Expectation of familiar, consistent care.
service_gaps.perception: Frequent rotation of midwives, feeling like a 'case number'.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'primary midwife' models or structured continuity-focused handover protocols.

service_gaps.expectation: Desire for autonomy and agency in decision making.
service_gaps.perception: Feeling coerced by 'clinical' leaflets and fear/pain, leading to compliance.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Develop trauma-informed decision support tools that address emotional safety alongside clinical data.

innovation_opportunities.description: Trauma-informed digital intake/profile tool that securely communicates sensitive history (trauma triggers) to rotating staff so the patient doesn't have to repeat it.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I had to explain my trauma history over and over again. Not the details... but just enough so they'd understand why I freeze up.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Nobody's ever really sat down with me and been like, 'Okay, what are your actual barriers?'

innovation_opportunities.description: A structured 'Birth Preferences' conversation guide that prioritizes psychological triggers and safety cues to ensure midwives actively honor patient boundaries during labor.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: That moment right there — that's when I'd need someone to see me. To actually see me.

expectations.text: I thought that by now, medicine would be more human.
I imagined a partnership. I wanted...

perceptions.text: Most of the time in here, I feel like a line item on a ledger rather than a person.
It’s rarely the same person. It’s like a carousel of faces—a revolving door of medical professionals.
The nurse looked at me like I was making things unnecessarily difficult... It felt like I was being managed, not cared for.

valued_items.text: She just placed a cool cloth on my forehead and held my hand for a moment... It wasn’t a medical intervention; it was just... humanity.
Finding someone else who felt 'processed' instead of 'cared for' makes me feel a little less crazy. It validates that my frustration isn't just me being 'difficult'.

missing_items.text: I wanted a witness, someone who cared about the experience of my transition into motherhood, but I only had experts interested in the mechanics of it.
I wanted choices that made me feel empowered, but all I was steered towards were choices that made their job easier.

service_gaps.expectation: Partnership and collaborative care.
service_gaps.perception: One-way directives and bureaucratic management of progress.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement structured collaborative care planning tools that prioritize maternal preferences alongside clinical benchmarks.

service_gaps.expectation: Emotional support and continuity of care.
service_gaps.perception: Rotational staff with a focus on clinical efficiency over relationship building.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Assign a dedicated 'lead midwife' or advocate to bridge transitions between rotational clinical staff.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a peer-mentoring or 'continuity of support' platform for single mothers to mitigate isolation.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Finding someone else who felt 'processed' instead of 'cared for' makes me feel a little less crazy.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a 'Personalized Birth Preference Tool' that translates clinical choices into non-medical, empowerment-focused language.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: The language is so detached, so clinical. It tells me what will happen, but never why or how I might actually feel about it.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: The lighting was the worst part—those buzzing, overhead fluorescents that stripped away any softness.

expectations.text: I want to feel human, but it’s hard when the system is built to make you feel like a product on an assembly line.
I want to be talked to like a human being, not a file, and that if things became difficult, I needed them to pause and explain why before they did anything to me.

perceptions.text: Everything was so fast, so clinical. There was no room for me to breathe or think, just people shouting protocols at me.
It’s exhausting. It’s like trying to build a house on sand—every time I start to feel even a tiny bit comfortable, the ground shifts.

valued_items.text: I need someone to look me in the eye, take my hand—not to check my pulse, but just to hold it—and tell me my name.

missing_items.text: I’m the one who has to carry the burden of continuity, not them.
There was no space for me to say, 'What would this mean for my life?' or 'How do I process this alone?'

service_gaps.expectation: Personalized care and being seen as a human.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a 'case' or 'product on an assembly line'.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Relationship-based midwifery models that reduce case loads.

service_gaps.expectation: Continuity of care and relationship building.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need someone to pause. I need someone to look me in the eye, take my hand—not to check my pulse, but just to hold it—and tell me my name.

perceptions.text: My midwife is wonderful, actually... She treats me like a colleague... I appreciate that. I don't need things dumbed down.
There are moments where I catch myself wishing she'd just... mother me a little... without the caveats.

valued_items.text: Sarah always makes space for him, asks his questions, includes him.
She explains things at this technical level, and I appreciate that.

missing_items.text: What I wish is that they'd ask more about how I'm doing emotionally. Not just the clinical stuff.
I haven't really leaned on other moms or support groups... I'm not ready to be vulnerable with people who don't understand the clinical side.

service_gaps.perception: Midwife defaultly treats her as a technical peer due to professional background.

perceptions.text: It feels like nobody actually knows you. This midwife called about a result... she was reading from my chart like she was meeting me for the first time.

valued_items.text: There's one midwife... who, when I mentioned my anxiety about this pregnancy, she said, 'That makes sense. You know too much and not enough at the same time.'

missing_items.text: I think I would've benefited from actually talking through it more deeply — not the medical facts, but the why of my choice.
No one who knows me well enough to say, 'Evette, that's normal worry' versus 'that's something we should monitor.'

service_gaps.expectation: Expectation of continuity to build trust and monitor emotional health.
service_gaps.perception: Fragmented care across multiple providers; focus on clinical efficiency over patient history.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement a shared digital care passport that highlights both clinical risks and emotional support needs across all provider touchpoints.

service_gaps.expectation: Needs-based emotional support for complex clinical decisions.
service_gaps.perception: Purely data-driven/clinical information provision.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They don't know that if I go quiet, it doesn't mean I'm fine — it means I'm spiraling internally.

perceptions.text: Every time someone spoke about me to another provider while I was right there, I felt erased. I felt like a piece of furniture that was getting in the way of the procedure.
The moments I felt supported were almost painfully small... She didn't treat me like a colleague or a liability; she treated me like a mother.

valued_items.text: That one sentence shattered the wall I’d been building. It allowed me to stop being the 'colleague' and finally let myself be the patient.
I needed to be treated with enough respect to be part of the loop.

missing_items.text: I felt like I was back in the breakroom, trying to hold my own against people who viewed me as just another file on the chart.
I didn't want a 'baby-sized fruit' comparison; I wanted to know about placental perfusion and statistical outcomes.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I didn't want a 'baby-sized fruit' comparison; I wanted to know about placental perfusion and statistical outcomes.

expectations.text: I just want to be heard, not managed.

perceptions.text: When you’re seeing a different person every few weeks, you become a case file, not a person.

valued_items.text: I need someone to look me in the eye and say, 'I know you're scared, I'm right here with you, and you're safe.'
I want to be heard, not managed.

missing_items.text: If I don't have a relationship with the provider, how am I supposed to trust their interpretation of the data?

service_gaps.perception: Standardized, episodic care by rotating strangers.

expectations.text: I pictured us as a team — him coming home from work, taking the older kids, changing diapers, making dinner while I tried to rest and heal.
I imagined late-night whispers over the baby monitor, the two of us trading smiles and small moments of normalcy.
I needed him to be calm, steady, and present — especially with my pre-eclampsia making every appointment scary.

perceptions.text: He’s tried — he stayed the night at the hospital once, brought me coffee, sat with me while they watched my blood pressure — and those moments meant everything.
But work and our tight money situation pull him away. He’s exhausted, sometimes short-tempered, and we snap at each other over little things.
Caregiving gets shared more on practical tasks than emotional labor; I still carry most of the worry. Intimacy feels distant; fear and fatigue eat up the space where closeness used to be.

valued_items.text: He’s tried — he stayed the night at the hospital once, brought me coffee, sat with me while they watched my blood pressure — and those moments meant everything.

some days I feel very alone even when he’s in the same room.

perceptions.text: The staff here has been really good, though. They don't make me feel rushed or judged for being young and unmarried.

valued_items.text: I didn't want a clinical consultation; I wanted someone to just *know* I was at my limit.
