service_gaps.perception: Rigid adherence to 'standard policy' requiring in-person visits.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement telehealth triaging for rural/solo parents to reduce the physical burden of travel for acute concerns.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Social Vulnerability' screening tool in maternity records to identify solo parents and those without support systems.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wish they had asked, 'Who is your village?' and when I said 'I don't really have one,' they hadn't just nodded.

innovation_opportunities.description: Virtual facilitated support groups specifically for solo/rural mothers, moderated by healthcare professionals to offer both empathy and clinical safety.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I needed someone to tell me it was okay to be exhausted and that I was doing a good job.

innovation_opportunities.description: Home-based or mobile midwifery visits for the first month postpartum to bridge the gap between hospital discharge and clinical follow-ups.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Instead of discharging me into the woods with a handful of pamphlets, I needed a system that checked in on me.

expectations.text: I went into this pregnancy with this deep, stubborn hope that my birth could be a collaborative, human experience.
I imagined myself sitting here by the pine trees, breastfeeding in a state of blissful, holistic recovery.

perceptions.text: It felt like a relentless cycle that kept me tethered to the house. When you’re isolated... the baby’s need to eat becomes this all-consuming clock you’re trapped under.
The provider was so clinical... They checked my blood pressure, asked if I was healing, and checked the baby’s growth, but the whole experience was hollow.

valued_items.text: I needed someone to sit with me, to hear me say it was hard, and to normalize the fact that it was stripping me of my autonomy.

missing_items.text: I needed community support, not just lactation advice.
I wanted to sit with my provider and talk about the emotional weight, the shift in my identity, and the isolation.

service_gaps.expectation: Anticipated a partnership where the mother's expertise on her body was respected.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a 'low-risk' statistic; autonomy ignored by clinical checklists.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'Person-Centered Goal Setting' protocols in maternity care.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected postnatal care to provide emotional debriefing and support for identity transition.
service_gaps.perception: Appointments were rapid-fire, clinical checklists that ignored emotional/social reality.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Introduce 'Holistic Postpartum Wellness' sessions that prioritize mental health and social support over solely clinical vitals.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'Patient Navigator' role specifically for rural, solo-parenting, or 'low-risk' mothers to bridge clinical care and social support.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I would move us toward a model where every person... is assigned a dedicated advocate—a navigator, if you will—whose only job is to bridge the gap between the clinical and the personal.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a virtual community platform specifically for rural postpartum parents to mitigate social isolation and provide peer-based emotional validation.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I needed community support, not just lactation advice. I needed someone to say, 'I know you're alone out there... and that is fundamentally exhausting.'

expectations.text: I thought I had the 'script' down. I’ve spent years working in holistic care—I know the anatomy, the physiology, the advocacy side of things.
We assumed it would be an even split, like it had eventually become with the others.

perceptions.text: The care I received was clinically perfect, don't get me wrong. My midwives are wonderful women.
The system treated me as a knowledgeable consumer rather than a person in transition... they didn't offer the kind of gentle, probing care that a first-time mother might get.

valued_items.text: He just sat on the floor with me, took the baby, and said, 'You don't have to be the expert today. You just have to be here.'
I found myself gravitating toward small, closed groups on platforms like Signal or private Facebook circles, specifically those for birth workers and midwives who are also mothers.

missing_items.text: I was desperate for someone to acknowledge the emotional tax of that birth. Most of the focus was on the baby’s weight gain or my physical healing.
I wish they had realized that being an expert doesn’t mean you’re immune to the vulnerability of being a mother.

service_gaps.expectation: Anticipated a partnership of equals regarding household/caregiving responsibilities.
service_gaps.perception: Felt she became the 'lead' and her partner the 'support staff' executing her manual.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Co-designed 'Postpartum Partnership' tools that facilitate shared cognitive load rather than unilateral management.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected care providers to address her as a whole person/mother.
service_gaps.perception: Providers deferred to her professional status and limited engagement to clinical check-boxes.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implementing identity-based screening alongside clinical vitals to uncover 'expert' status bias.

innovation_opportunities.description: Non-clinical, facilitated debrief sessions at 4-6 weeks postpartum focused on birth narrative and identity transition, independent of physical exams.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: If I could pick one single change... it would be the inclusion of a dedicated, non-clinical 'debrief' session around four to six weeks postpartum.

innovation_opportunities.description: Professional-peer support networks for maternity care workers/providers to safely drop the 'expert' mask.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Those spaces were different from the broader, more filtered social media feeds. They were useful in a way my clinical check-ups never were.

expectations.text: I expected that since I had an uncomplicated, unmedicated birth, I’d be back on my feet in no time
I expected that because we’d talked so much about our values and my birth plan, the caregiving would naturally just be a 50/50 split of labor
I expected a partnership that would honor my autonomy and my desire for a physiological, unmedicated birth without treating me like a clinical anomaly.

perceptions.text: The support I was offered felt very... clinical. It was all about mechanics
It was efficient, sure, but it felt incredibly superficial... they missed the woman sitting right in front of them
Returning to work... felt like I was splitting myself in two. One moment I’m nursing... the next, I’m trying to engage in high-level discussions

valued_items.text: I didn't need a textbook; I needed to be heard.
I wanted someone to say, 'I know you know all the facts, but how are you holding up?'

missing_items.text: We need to do better at acknowledging that the transition to motherhood is a massive, life-altering event, not just a medical procedure
I needed someone to validate that it’s okay for a seasoned birth worker to feel overwhelmed and insecure

service_gaps.expectation: Professional/clinical partnership recognizing my expertise while supporting my vulnerability.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a clinical peer/test case where technical knowledge masked the need for emotional support.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Redesign postnatal interactions to prioritize 'witnessing' over 'checking'.

service_gaps.expectation: Expectation that postpartum physical recovery would be swift and heroic due to knowledge.
service_gaps.perception: Reality of exhaustion and hormone shifts made the 'expert' identity feel like a restrictive mask.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Shift focus from purely physical recovery markers to mental health resilience screening.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement 'Witnessing Sessions' in postnatal care that explicitly decouple emotional support from clinical check-lists.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: You don't want a consultant; you want a witness.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a 'Postpartum Mental Transition' toolkit that validates feelings of identity loss and professional-to-mother dissonance.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’m less concerned with being the 'right' kind of mother and more interested in just being the 'real' one.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a peer-support platform for 'Professional Mothers' to normalize the dissonance of returning to work while still in physical/emotional recovery.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I felt like if I dropped the 'professional' mask, I’d lose the respect I’d worked so hard to earn.

expectations.text: I wanted someone to stop looking at my charts and start looking at me.
I wanted us to be partners, but I’ve accidentally turned myself into a manager and him into an employee.

perceptions.text: My midwife is kind, but she moves through my check-ups so quickly because she assumes I’ve got it all handled.
The care I received was perfectly adequate by the medical standards—I was physically healthy, the baby was hitting her markers—but it was hollow.

valued_items.text: That space is valuable because it isn't 'clinical.' When I say I’m struggling, they don’t ask for my stats. They just validate the mess.

missing_items.text: I wish my midwife or my primary care provider had sat me down... and said, 'Herminia, I know what you do for a living, but today, you are the patient.'
I needed someone to sit with me and say, 'It’s okay that you’re exhausted, and it’s okay that you’re scared.'

service_gaps.expectation: Needs to be seen as a vulnerable patient rather than an expert colleague.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a peer/expert who needs no 'hand-holding', resulting in isolation.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Provider 'de-professionalization' training for clinicians-as-patients.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'De-professionalization Protocol' for healthcare workers accessing maternity services to explicitly strip away the colleague-role and enforce patient status.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wish my midwife or my primary care provider had sat me down in that very first visit and said, 'Herminia, I know what you do for a living, but today, you are the patient.'

innovation_opportunities.description: Create 'peer-witnessing' spaces that deliberately bypass clinical data sharing to focus on emotional validation.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: What makes this space useful is the lack of hierarchy... when I say I’m struggling, they don’t ask for my stats. They just validate the mess.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a structured emotional-health screening tool that includes questions about the 'identity shift' and parental-professional friction, specifically designed for high-achieving patients.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: The system is designed to catch medical complications, but it’s woefully equipped to catch the kind of quiet, mounting despair that comes from feeling like you’re failing.

expectations.text: I had this idea that because I knew the anatomy, because I knew the physiology of healing, I would be able to troubleshoot my own body like a patient.
I expected the check-ups to be a sanctuary. I thought they would be the one place where I could set down the mantle of 'professional' and actually be heard as a person.

perceptions.text: The appointments were brief, surgical, and efficient... It was high-quality clinical care, technically, but it felt emotionally sterile.
My husband is incredibly supportive—he is kind, he is present, and he tries so hard.

valued_items.text: I wanted someone to say, 'I know you know the science, but how does this feel in your bones?'

missing_items.text: I needed someone to bypass my professional persona and just ask me how I was actually sleeping, and if I was ever truly alone with my own thoughts.

service_gaps.expectation: Midwifery check-ups as a sanctuary for vulnerability and emotional support.
service_gaps.perception: Clinically efficient, checklist-based appointments that ignored the mother's mental state.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Redesign postnatal appointments to include mandatory, non-clinical wellness check-ins.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional knowledge would serve as a buffer against postpartum emotional struggle.
service_gaps.perception: Professional identity acted as a barrier, preventing caregivers from offering necessary emotional support.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'caregiver-as-patient' protocols that de-prioritize expertise during consultations.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'Hidden Needs' screening protocol for healthcare professionals who are also patients to bypass their professional persona.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I needed someone to bypass my professional persona and just ask me how I was actually sleeping.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a specialized postpartum support track focused on the 'how-to' of transitioning back to a professional role while maintaining mental health.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: We spend so much time teaching parents the 'how-to' of the baby... but we almost entirely neglect the 'how-to' of being a new mother who is also a professional.

expectations.text: I thought there would be acknowledgment that I was a single mother this time. That maybe they would ask more questions, offer more resources.
I thought it would be like the textbooks say... good prenatal visits, education about recovery, maybe someone checking in on you.

perceptions.text: The visits felt rushed. The midwife or doctor would come, do their check very quickly, and then they were gone.
They gave me papers with instructions, but nobody helped me think about how I would actually do these things.
The lactation consultant I saw, she was nice, but she only came once.

valued_items.text: That small thing — someone seeing me, acknowledging I existed — it broke through something.
What I needed most was someone to say, 'How will you actually do this?' and then help me figure it out.

missing_items.text: Did they ask how I would get to the next appointment? No. Did they ask if I had someone to help? Not really.
I needed someone to see me. Not just as a patient with checkboxes, but as a person who is alone now.

service_gaps.expectation: Anticipated proactive support and acknowledgment of single-parent status.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a patient with checkboxes; support was reactive and task-focused.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement a 'whole-person' intake assessment that screens for social determinants of health during pregnancy.

service_gaps.expectation: Clear, accessible instructions and follow-up support.
service_gaps.perception: Received static papers without planning help for logistical barriers like transport.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Develop a 'Postpartum Logistics Plan' co-created by a care navigator to identify transit and support gaps before discharge.

innovation_opportunities.description: Designated Care Navigation role to bridge the gap between medical clearance and daily life, focusing on non-clinical barriers like transport and childcare.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Someone — just one person — who could coordinate my care. Someone who knew about transportation... who could connect me to resources.

innovation_opportunities.description: Telehealth/Virtual support groups specifically for single mothers or high-risk postpartum patients available during evening hours.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: The support groups they mentioned — they are during the day when I am working, or in parts of town I cannot reach.

innovation_opportunities.description: Standardized screening for 'health literacy anxiety' in postpartum women with medical backgrounds to provide validation and targeted emotional support.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I know the infection rates... I know the worst-case scenarios. And nobody helped me process that.

expectations.text: I thought I would be more prepared this time... but it's different when you're alone.
I thought my body would know what to do [with breastfeeding].
I expected to feel joy. Instead, I felt trapped.
I expected continuity of care.

perceptions.text: The support they offered... it wasn't for someone like me.
She never asked about my mental health. She never asked if I was coping.
They speak slowly, use simple words. They don't know that I understand the science.
I felt like I've disappeared. There's just the mother now.

valued_items.text: It's nice that someone wants to listen, actually.
Someone who knows me, who follows me from pregnancy through the first year.

missing_items.text: What I needed was someone to come to my house. To see me actually trying to feed him.
I needed them to ask about my situation. To understand that I'm alone.
No one offered solutions [for transportation].

service_gaps.expectation: Support for a successful recovery and feeding.
service_gaps.perception: Handed a pamphlet during business hours when she is working.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Home visiting services or asynchronous telehealth.

service_gaps.expectation: A clinical environment that recognizes professional expertise.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as if she does not understand science/simple language.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Patient-centered intake forms that record professional/educational background.

service_gaps.expectation: Assessment of holistic needs including mental health.
service_gaps.perception: A 10-minute physical exam with a checkbox form.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: In-person, dedicated mental health check-ins beyond standard forms.

innovation_opportunities.description: Mobile/Home visiting lactation and clinical support for mothers without reliable transport.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They could have offered home visits.

innovation_opportunities.description: Standardized 'Social Determinants of Health' screening at the first prenatal visit to tailor care plans.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: the system needs to know your actual situation... do you have transportation? Do you have childcare?

innovation_opportunities.description: Dedicated, sanitary, and private pumping spaces in workplace/clinical settings.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I use the bathroom sometimes, but that's not sanitary.

innovation_opportunities.description: Continuity-of-care model assigning one primary provider/case manager throughout the first year.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: One person who knows me, who follows me from pregnancy through the first year.

innovation_opportunities.description: The transcript provided is empty. Please provide the participant's responses to the interview questions to enable a full analysis of expectations, perceptions, and innovation opportunities.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: N/A

expectations.text: I want to be treated with respect by my doctors, to feel listened to, not rushed or dismissed because my English is not perfect.
I want [my husband] to be part of appointments so he can help speak for us when I’m shy.
I want less fear and more support, and help finding steady work so I can care for children without constant worry.

valued_items.text: He already comforts me, telling me be patient
the right vaccines at the right time, a warm home

missing_items.text: help finding steady work so I can care for children without constant worry.

service_gaps.expectation: To be treated with respect and not dismissed due to language barriers.
service_gaps.perception: Anticipated risk of being rushed or dismissed based on past/general experience.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement language-concordant care or translation support protocols.

service_gaps.expectation: Husband as an active participant to help with communication.
service_gaps.perception: Unmet barrier to fully integrating partner into appointment flow.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Standardize 'shared visit' intake forms that explicitly invite partner presence.

innovation_opportunities.description: Integration of social prescribing (e.g., employment/financial counseling) into maternity intake.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: help finding steady work so I can care for children without constant worry.

innovation_opportunities.description: Multilingual patient advocacy tools to ensure comprehension during consultations.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: not rushed or dismissed because my English is not perfect.

expectations.text: What I’m looking for is someone to just look me in the eye and acknowledge that I am terrified. I wanted a partner in this, not just a technician.
I wanted someone who would sit with me for an extra minute when they see my hands shaking as they wrap the cuff, instead of just scribbling numbers.

perceptions.text: The doctors are clinically brilliant—I’m alive because they know their stuff—but they treat the trauma like it’s a separate, inconvenient file.
The office is cold and clinical, designed to move people through like a conveyor belt. There’s no room for the fear.

valued_items.text: It’s been the one nurse at the clinic—let’s call her Sarah—who actually pauses. She doesn’t just come in with the cuff and the clipboard; she asks, 'How are you doing, really?'
The cold, hard data is sometimes easier to handle than someone’s flowery, 'everything will be perfect' birth story.

missing_items.text: I think if someone—even once—had asked, 'How does it feel to be in your body right now, given everything you’ve been through?' it might have changed everything.
I want the person sticking the needle in my arm to look me in the face and ask, 'Are you okay today?' and actually wait for the answer.

service_gaps.expectation: Acknowledgment of trauma and emotional partnership.
service_gaps.perception: Clinical management focused solely on physical metrics and 'ticking boxes'.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrate trauma-informed care protocols into routine physical assessments.

service_gaps.expectation: Validation of bodily symptoms and patient expertise.
service_gaps.perception: Dismissal of concerns as 'stress' or 'anxiety' based on past clinical stability.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Structured 'Patient-Reported Symptom' validation tool that triggers a mandatory pause/assessment.

innovation_opportunities.description: Integrated 'Mental Health Check-in' at the point of care.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I don't want to be sent to a separate office down the hall to talk about my feelings. I want the person sticking the needle in my arm to look me in the face and ask, 'Are you okay today?'

innovation_opportunities.description: Trauma-informed digital 'Emergency Narrative' record.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’m basically building a digital archive of my own risk... so that someone will finally believe me when I say I knew something was coming.

expectations.text: I wanted them to see the person who survived that first experience, not just a chart flagged with 'High Risk' in red ink.
I expected a partnership, but I mostly feel like a passenger in a car where I don't know who’s driving.
I envisioned a primary provider—a midwife or a doctor who would become a fixture in my life.

perceptions.text: It’s sterile. It’s efficient. It’s all heart rate monitors, urine samples, and those brisk, clinical nods.
It’s a revolving door. I see a different face almost every time I go to the clinic.
Don’t get me wrong, they’re excellent at what they do—they catch the numbers, they adjust the meds.

valued_items.text: I just want to feel like I’m going to make it to the finish line this time.
I need someone to act as a bridge between the clinical data and the reality of my daily life.

missing_items.text: There’s no 'we' in the process. It’s 'the results are fine,' and then I’m back in the parking lot.
I need someone to say, 'I know you've been through hell, and it makes sense that you're terrified.'

service_gaps.expectation: I wanted them to see the person who survived that first experience.
service_gaps.perception: My medical record had effectively swallowed my personhood. When they see 'eclampsia' in my file, they don't see the woman.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Biopsychosocial profiling integration into EHR.

service_gaps.expectation: I envisioned a primary provider who would become a fixture in my life.
service_gaps.perception: It’s a revolving door. I see a different face almost every time I go to the clinic.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Primary midwifery lead model for high-risk patients.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Trauma-Informed Care Passport' that automatically flags the patient's psychological history to all new providers.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Every single time, I have to recount my history—the eclampsia, the trauma, the fear—as if I’m reading a script to a stranger.

innovation_opportunities.description: Designate a 'Care Navigator' role specifically for high-risk, trauma-affected patients to bridge the gap between clinical appointments.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need someone to act as a bridge between the clinical data and the reality of my daily life.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create 'Intermediate Support' protocols for high-risk patients to call when they are not in crisis but are experiencing significant anxiety.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: There’s no space for the in-between—the moments where I’m not dying but I am spiraling.

expectations.text: I wanted to find a space where I could set down the 'educator' persona and just be a patient, someone who is held and cared for rather than someone who is constantly analyzing the care being given.
I want her to tell me that everything is okay with a level of confidence that makes me feel like I can stop hyper-analyzing every sensation.

perceptions.text: The clinical side is handled flawlessly—my blood pressure is checked, my baby is measured, and everything is by the book. But the emotional side? It’s often left to me to navigate.
I often feel like I’ve attended a professional consultation rather than a prenatal visit.

valued_items.text: You’re here as a mom today. Your baby is doing great, and for the next twenty minutes, you don’t need to know the 'why' or the 'how.'
She just walked over, put a hand on my shoulder, and kept it there while we listened to the heartbeat together.

missing_items.text: I’ve never had a provider ask me, 'How do you feel about these changes?' or 'How are you adjusting to your body feeling like it belongs to someone else for a while?'
There isn't a specific support structure for a patient who is also a peer.

service_gaps.expectation: To be treated as a vulnerable patient rather than a professional peer.
service_gaps.perception: Providers default to clinical shorthand because they assume my background means I don't need comfort.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Provider communication training on 'colleague-patient' bias.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implementation of a 'Care Preference Profile' where patients can explicitly state their desired communication style (e.g., 'Treat me as a parent, not an expert').
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want someone to look at me and see the mother, not the fertility educator.

innovation_opportunities.description: Training modules for clinicians on recognizing and de-escalating anxiety in highly informed patients without reverting to raw data.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: She started rattling off statistics and differential diagnoses... It was the exact opposite of what I needed.

innovation_opportunities.description: Creation of peer-support 'vulnerability circles' specifically for health professionals navigating pregnancy to offload the 'expert' persona.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I think the system is designed to provide information, but it isn't designed to hold space for someone who already has too much information.

expectations.text: I expected to walk into the office and feel that same professional distance I’d spent my career cultivating.
I pictured myself having an objective, data-driven conversation... a crisp, efficient delivery of information.
I wanted that “midwife-led” ideal where we’d move past the formalities of my chart and reach a point where she knew not just my risk factors, but me.

perceptions.text: When I actually walked in for that first visit... the environment felt… overwhelming. I expected to feel empowered by my knowledge, but I actually felt exposed.
My midwife is wonderful, but sometimes she gives me this look—like she’s wondering if I’m waiting for her to say something specific... It makes me want to scream.
The response has been efficient but sterile... It’s almost always filtered through administrative hurdles.

valued_items.text: He’ll just put a hand on my back or change the subject entirely to something mundane, like the nursery paint or what the older kids are doing.
I need someone to say, 'Dulcie, I know you know all the risks, but your body is doing exactly what it's supposed to do.'

missing_items.text: What’s missing is the permission to be something other than an expert.
I need a human connection that acknowledges how hard it is to turn off the clinical part of my brain.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional consultation as a peer/expert.
service_gaps.perception: Feelings of vulnerability and being a 'specimen' to be managed.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Provider sensitivity training for high-knowledge patients.

service_gaps.expectation: Efficient data-driven exchange of info.
service_gaps.perception: Isolation caused by clinical sterility; craving for human reassurance.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Balanced information delivery that includes emotional validation.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a 'Patient Persona' flag in electronic records for healthcare professionals to adapt communication style for high-knowledge patients (e.g., healthcare workers) who need emotional support over technical data.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need a human connection that acknowledges how hard it is to turn off the clinical part of my brain.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'check-in' protocol at the start of appointments that prioritizes emotional status over biological markers (blood pressure, etc.) to normalize vulnerability.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want someone to notice that I’m tired, or that I’m nervous, rather than just marking “normal” in my chart.

expectations.text: I wanted to feel like my questions were valid, even if they seemed 'alternative' or whatever.
I wanted continuity. That's huge for me. I don't want to explain my whole situation... to a different person every single appointment.
I just want to be treated like a real person. Like a partner in my own care, not just... a case.

perceptions.text: One doctor kind of dismissed my questions about induction protocols by saying, 'Well, let's not worry about that yet,' which felt like... I don't know, like my reading wasn't legitimate?
The appointments are quick. The providers are professional, sure, but nobody asks how I'm doing. Like, emotionally.
The nurse just... laughed. Actually laughed and said, 'You're overthinking this.'

valued_items.text: It's... it's nice to be asked, you know? Like, my opinion matters.
I want to understand the actual evidence, not just someone's opinion dressed up as fact.

missing_items.text: I've never had anyone sit with me and say, 'This is hard, and your feelings about that are valid.'
No explanation of why, no discussion of what it meant if something came back abnormal, nothing.

service_gaps.expectation: Expectation of partnership and active listening from providers.
service_gaps.perception: Providers dismiss questions and use 'paternalism' to manage patients as cases.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Redesign patient-provider interactions to include structured 'listening' check-ins.

service_gaps.expectation: Continuity of care to avoid repeating life/work situation context.
service_gaps.perception: Rotational care with different providers who do not acknowledge personal context.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Digital health passport that persists context across clinical encounters.

innovation_opportunities.description: Evidence-based decision-aid tool that allows patients to input their preferences before visits to facilitate partnership.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want to be an informed partner in my care, not so I could reject her expertise.

innovation_opportunities.description: Emotional health check-in protocol that is distinct from clinical measurements.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Nobody asked me what I already knew. Nobody said, 'Tell me what you've learned.'

innovation_opportunities.description: Standardized 'shared-knowledge' documentation system to prevent patient labeling (e.g., 'non-compliant') and record patient preferences.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I saw it at the next appointment. Non-standard. Like wanting to move during labor... was somehow outside the realm of reasonable preferences.

expectations.text: I expected it to be more... celebratory? Like, I know that sounds naive, but I thought there'd be this moment where someone would be like, 'Congratulations, let's talk about what you need.'
I expected that if I had a question or concern, I could call and get a same-day or next-day response.
I went in thinking I'd have maybe one or two providers who'd know me throughout this whole thing.

perceptions.text: Instead, the first appointment felt like a checklist. They did the blood work, asked a bunch of rapid-fire questions, and I felt rushed the whole time.

The reality was... different. I got a call from someone in the office I'd never met before. She said my screening came back 'normal' but in this way that made me feel like I should already know what that means?
I had one appointment with a nurse practitioner—not even my main doctor—and she asked me what my concerns were before she told me anything. It was such a small thing, but it felt huge.

valued_items.text: I want to understand my options. I want my doctors to see me as someone who's thought about this, not just another statistic.

missing_items.text: I didn't get the actual numbers, the ranges, what they were screening for specifically.

valued_items.text: I trust the science books the most—the ones with the pictures of how the organs form. Biology is a language that does not change.

valued_items.text: She just put her hand on my shoulder for a second and asked, 'How are you really doing?'

The reality is way more fragmented... My OB mentioned the elevated blood pressure... and then I had to wait three days for results... I got a phone call — a phone call — from a nurse.

valued_items.text: She took me seriously. I've heard stories from other women about doctors brushing off their concerns... That didn't happen to me, and I'm grateful for that.

perceptions.text: The doctors are professional, I’ll give them that, but it feels like a script. They come in, check the numbers on the monitor, nod, and leave. It’s like they’re managing a malfunctioning machine rather than caring for a person.
The environment was a stark, sterile machine designed for efficiency, not for human beings in the throes of one of life's most vulnerable moments.

valued_items.text: She didn't look at her clipboard first. She just pulled the stool up really close, took a breath, and said, 'Clarinda, before we look at the chart, how are you actually holding up?'

He waited for a lull in the noise and said, 'I know the room feels like it's spinning. You’re doing the work of ten people right now. You’re doing a good job, and we’re going to get you through this.'

missing_items.text: They warn you about stretch marks and heartburn, but they don't prepare you for the feeling of being a prisoner in your own skin.
There was no space for the fear, the grief for the pregnancy I thought I was going to have, or the humiliation of being so utterly dependent on machines.

valued_items.text: My first pregnancy actually had this one nurse midwife who came in and just... sat with me for a minute. Didn't pull up my chart right away. Asked me about my son, about how I was feeling about doing this again.
