perceptions.text: And the system needs to recognize that one-size-fits-all screening protocols don't work when you have a diverse population with different needs.
The sonographer probably thought I wanted the data, not the comfort. And part of me leaned into that because it felt safer—more professional, less vulnerable.

valued_items.text: If I had one midwife—someone who knew my history, who understood my baseline anxiety... I could actually be honest with them.
I need someone to treat me like I deserve support, not like I'm a competent professional who should be able to manage on her own.

missing_items.text: I don't have anyone to reality-check me anymore... someone to hold me when I'm scared.
What I wish is that someone would ask me what I actually want to know, and what I don't.

service_gaps.perception: Automated, clinical-only interactions that pathologize or ignore emotional needs.

service_gaps.expectation: Continuity of care to build a trusted relationship.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: What I wish is that someone would ask me what I actually want to know, and what I don't.

expectations.text: I thought I was prepared, you know? I know the statistics, the protocols, what can go wrong.

perceptions.text: Dr. Morrison is competent, thorough. She answers my questions without making me feel like I'm wasting her time.

valued_items.text: I need someone to tell me it's okay to just be a pregnant woman sometimes, not a medical professional having a pregnancy.
There's no judgment there. [Online communities]

missing_items.text: What I'm really missing is someone who can sit with the emotional weight of this alongside the medical facts.

service_gaps.expectation: Expectation of a collaborative, patient-centered care partnership.

service_gaps.expectation: Healthcare providers would recognize and respect her professional autonomy.
service_gaps.perception: Clinical background causes providers to dismiss or minimize her emotional needs, labeling her 'difficult'.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Provider sensitivity training regarding 'patient-professional' dual-identity challenges.

valued_items.text: Someone to say, 'I know you're scared, and it's okay that you're scared,' that would change everything.
Someone who helps me translate my own feelings into the language the doctors use.

missing_items.text: I have this notebook where I write down my questions... but when I get to the clinic, it’s always someone new.

She didn't just rush... she just put her hand on my shoulder. She didn't ask me a question, she just waited. That felt important.
When they touch my belly—the cold gel, the pressure of the probe—I feel like my body doesn't belong to me.

valued_items.text: It’s the first time someone asked me what I feel, not just how my blood pressure is.
I looked for posts from other women who felt lost or who were doing this alone. I looked for people who used words that sounded like my own struggles.

missing_items.text: I wish someone—just one person—had sat me down at the start and said, 'Here is how this works, and here is how we can help you.'

expectations.text: I want them to see that I'm careful, that I care about my baby.
I just want to feel like they see me as a good mother who wants the best for her children.

Every time I call the clinic, it's a different voice... They see you like a number. Another appointment, another result to report.

valued_items.text: I needed her to look at me and say, 'This is normal, don't worry,' instead of just calling the doctor like something was wrong.
When you see the same midwife each time, she knows you. She knows you're scared. She knows you have questions.

missing_items.text: I wish she had given me a paper to read at home. I wish she had said, 'Take time to think about this.'

service_gaps.expectation: Informed, unpressured choice.

perceptions.text: Sometimes I feel like I'm just a... a list of problems. They say 'medium risk' and I wonder if they mean because of my situation... or because something is actually wrong.

valued_items.text: She sat down and explained why. She said it protects the baby from pertussis before he's even born. Nobody had explained it like that before.
She sat right next to me during the ultrasound, and just having her there... I could breathe.

missing_items.text: There's no interpreter there... by the time they arrange it, the appointment is almost over.
Nobody has ever offered anything like that [support workers] to me... I have problems—finding steady work, managing three children—but I don't know who to ask.

service_gaps.perception: Feeling like a 'list of problems' with medical jargon that isn't explained.

service_gaps.expectation: Support for non-medical life stressors (work, childcare, isolation).
service_gaps.perception: Clinical staff only focus on a checklist of vitals/measurements.

expectations.text: I'm not going to sugarcoat it — this pregnancy has been nothing like I expected

perceptions.text: I'm getting these random phone calls from faceless midwives reading off results like they're reading a grocery list.

valued_items.text: I need someone to hold my hand, maybe. To say 'You're doing okay' even if I'm not. To be present with me in that fear
someone to sit with me and ask what I needed — not just hand me a consent form.

expectations.text: I want to be the 'good patient' who just does what she's told. But instead, I'm the one asking too many questions... I just need someone to help me feel like this will actually be okay.

perceptions.text: She's efficient. Professional. Which is good, but sometimes I leave appointments feeling like I've been processed rather than heard.

valued_items.text: She doesn't talk down to me, which I appreciate. When I come in with questions... she actually *engages* with the data instead of just telling me to stop worrying.

missing_items.text: What I really needed was someone who understood both the clinical side and trauma—someone who could sit between me and Dr. Patterson.

perceptions.text: instead of a parade of different midwives reciting test results like they're reading from a script.

expectations.text: Mostly I want someone to see the worry behind the perfect vitals and help me move from fear to trust.
I keep asking whether postpartum hormones might trigger another flare-up, or if that weird pain is nothing.

perceptions.text: My OB is great — very competent and kind — but I sometimes leave feeling like a checkbox rather than someone carrying a messy history.

valued_items.text: What helped me — or what I think would help — is a single person who sees that fear and names it back to me: 'You’re scared and that’s okay.'
being spoken to like a partner in the decision, not a passive body; being given a quick, clear explanation of options; being asked 'do you want more time?'

missing_items.text: Afterwards I desperately want a debrief — someone to explain what happened, why decisions were made, and to acknowledge the emotional toll.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Afterwards I need someone to say, 'You chose as best you could with what you knew,' and to explain why things went as they did.

innovation_opportunities.description: Digital 'pause button' feature for shared decision-making during labour to facilitate reflection time.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: being asked 'do you want more time?' and actually getting that time when safe.

expectations.text: I wrote that I wanted my husband to stay with me, always... because he is my translator, but also because he is my anchor.
I made this plan because I thought it would help them understand me — that I am not just a body having a baby, I am a person with thoughts and fears.

perceptions.text: The clinic here, it's good, I think. The doctors are kind.
She glances at it like it's... like it's a piece of trash. Like my wishes don't matter.
I was strapped to a table... I was just supposed to trust that it was necessary.

valued_items.text: That moment, it was... it was just ours... Just two people who made a life together. A moment of... still having hope.
If it was the same midwife, someone who knows me, who I have built trust with... maybe I could ask better questions.

missing_items.text: The interpreter, she doesn't always come, and my husband, he cannot leave work to translate for me every time.

service_gaps.perception: Husband was asked to leave the room for clinical procedures.

service_gaps.perception: Birth plan was disregarded and set aside by clinical staff.

innovation_opportunities.description: Designated 'Case Navigator' or consistent lead midwife program to prevent patients from 'falling through the cracks' across visits.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Everything falls through the cracks. It's like I am telling my story five times, but nobody is listening to the whole story.

expectations.text: I didn't expect to need [a receptionist] so much. In my mind, the doctor or midwife should be the person, you know? They have the medical knowledge.
I wish he could be there more [at appointments]. I wish that wasn't asking too much.

perceptions.text: The worst part is when the interpreter doesn't come. Then I just sit there, nodding, hoping I understand what they're saying.
The midwife I see most often... she is kind, I think. But she is always rushing, always looking at the clock.
She [Maria] treated me like a person, not a problem to solve quickly.

valued_items.text: She looked at my face and she saw I was frightened. And she said, very gently, 'Briana, I know this feels uncomfortable. You're doing great.'
She [Maria] sat down next to me for a few minutes. She asked how I was really doing, not just the clinic questions.

missing_items.text: There is no support worker for me. No one calls to check how I'm doing between visits.
I wish I could have my mother. I wish I could have Rosa. I wish someone would just... stay with me.

service_gaps.expectation: Expecting healthcare providers to facilitate understanding and provide clear communication.
service_gaps.perception: Providers speak in clinical jargon; interpreters are frequently absent or unavailable.

service_gaps.expectation: Systemic support for high-risk life stressors (financial/social).
service_gaps.perception: Isolation; no one is aware of non-medical barriers to care like lack of bus fare or childcare.

expectations.text: The thing I want most is to feel treated with dignity. Not like a problem to solve, but like a person who deserves to have agency in what happens to her own body.
I'm very careful about finding providers who actually ask before they do things. Who treat my no like it matters.

perceptions.text: I'm constantly re-explaining, re-advocating, starting from zero. I've had to keep my own mental notes about which provider is which... That's work I shouldn't have to do.
The leaflet made it sound straightforward, but I know it's not. I shouldn't have to crowdsource my own care.

valued_items.text: I appreciate that she's asking. That matters to me.
I need to feel like an active participant in what happens to my own body.

missing_items.text: Nobody's connected the dots about what my needs actually are. One midwife knows I want more time. Another knows I'm doing this alone... but there's no central understanding of me.
I need someone to acknowledge that this is hard... to narrate what they're doing. I need them to touch me like I'm a person, not a problem.

service_gaps.perception: Having to explain trauma and needs to multiple new providers every visit.

service_gaps.expectation: Informed consent involving detailed discussion of risks and options.
service_gaps.perception: Providers treating information as a checkbox or handing out leaflets without context.

valued_items.text: She explains what she's going to do before she does it, and she actually waits for me to say I'm ready.

valued_items.text: He didn't try to solve the clinical problem or recite medical statistics; he just anchored me to the present.

missing_items.text: I’m already on my third midwife, and at twenty-eight weeks, I’m craving continuity.

valued_items.text: When my husband is there, the room feels like a private space. He’s my boundary.

missing_items.text: I’ve had to ask for an extra session just to talk about my birth plan, and I could tell the scheduler was surprised.

valued_items.text: I just want someone to look at me and say, 'I remember you. I know how much you care.'

valued_items.text: A midwife who sat with me after the birth, no checklist, just held my hand.
The real need for practical help (meals, a late-night run for diapers, someone to watch the baby).

missing_items.text: Screening for mood is fine, but follow-up matters more than a form.
Maternity services acknowledged the ripple effects — the precariousness of work, the isolation of rural life.

service_gaps.expectation: I wanted permission to be scared and held.
service_gaps.perception: I felt alone even when people were technically 'there.'
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Training providers to assess emotional needs independent of professional background.

service_gaps.expectation: Care that sees the person beneath the resume.
service_gaps.perception: Everyone assumed I knew what I wanted and needed because I am a nurse.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Standardizing patient-centered intake that prioritizes personal emotional state over professional status.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Practical Support Referral' system that connects parents with local resources for physical needs (meals, chores) rather than just clinical check-ins.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I also wish maternity services acknowledged the ripple effects... the real need for practical help.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a 'Postnatal Follow-up Protocol' that triggers active support and home visits based on mood screenings, moving beyond digital forms.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Screening for mood is fine, but follow-up matters more than a form.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create clinical training modules specifically to address the 'professional bias' where healthcare workers acting as patients are denied standard emotional support.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Please stop assuming older, experienced women don’t need emotional coaching.

expectations.text: I really thought the staff would view [my birth plan] as a collaborative tool
I thought the support would be centered on my autonomy
I imagined I’d be welcomed with warmth, like they were excited to help me bring my second child into the world.
I expected my midwife to be my anchor.

perceptions.text: It felt like they were just being polite... just another piece of paperwork to get through.
The staff kept asking, 'What do you want to do?' and honestly, it felt like an interrogation instead of support.
Everything felt incredibly transactional. Nobody looked at me as a person bringing life into the world; I was just a chart number.
When the baby's heart rate dipped and they suggested pitocin... it didn’t feel like a discussion—it felt like a directive.

valued_items.text: I wanted to feel cared for, but instead, I just felt monitored.
I wanted to be the first one to truly welcome her
I needed someone to sit with me and say, 'I see that you're exhausted, and it’s okay if this feels hard.'

missing_items.text: I needed a person, and I was given a technician.
There was no space for the 'me' inside this shell [during check-ups].
I feel like I’m constantly trying to bring him into my internal world... and neither of us is succeeding.

service_gaps.expectation: Midwife to be an emotional anchor and bridge clinical/emotional needs.
service_gaps.perception: Midwife's attention was fractured; clinical role competed with emotional support.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implementing 'dedicated emotional support' roles or designated doula/support advocates within the clinical team.

service_gaps.expectation: Collaboration and shared decision-making regarding interventions.
service_gaps.perception: Directives were given with urgency; lack of agency and transparency.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Visual decision-aid tools for labor that present risks/benefits in non-emergency scenarios to ensure patient understanding.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'Trauma-Informed Postnatal Debriefing' program that goes beyond clinical vitals to address the patient's psychological narrative of birth.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wanted to talk about it. I wanted to see if there was some support for this specific kind of vigilance... she just did that polite, tight-lipped smile and said, 'That’s so normal.'

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop flexible monitoring technologies that allow for mobility and hydrotherapy without requiring patients to be 'anchored' to hospital beds.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wanted to be mobile, but the technology kept me anchored. It made me feel like my body was a piece of equipment that needed to be plugged in.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a 'Support Person Integration' framework to help partners understand how to provide psychological validation rather than just logistical problem-solving.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: He tries to 'fix' it... but it feels like he’s invalidating the depth of what I’m feeling.

expectations.text: I was just blissfully ignorant; I went with the flow because I didn’t know what I didn’t know.
I felt like if I stopped advocating or researching, I was failing to protect the baby.

perceptions.text: Having a provider who acknowledges your need for information—without making you feel like a 'difficult' patient—is everything.
It was like hitting a 'reset' button on my identity. I had to re-explain my background and my concerns.

valued_items.text: Sarah... didn't just brush my concerns off with a 'don't worry, you're low risk.' She actually walked me through the clinical reasoning.
The person who actually noticed was my sister... she just sat there and held the space for the fact that I wasn't doing okay.

missing_items.text: I wish there had been more space for the 'researcher' version of me to be understood, rather than just managed.
Nobody really stopped to look at me and ask if I was actually functioning.

service_gaps.expectation: Personalized, continuous care that accounts for individual anxiety levels.
service_gaps.perception: Interaction with GP/Health visitors felt like 'starting from scratch' and superficial check-ins.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrated longitudinal mental health tracking across maternity and pediatric services.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implementation of a 'Clinical Continuity Passport' or digital profile that tracks not just medical history, but specific patient anxiety triggers and preferred communication styles.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: It was like hitting a 'reset' button on my identity. I had to re-explain my background and my concerns.

innovation_opportunities.description: Standardized 'Psychosocial Check-in' protocols for all postnatal appointments, mandating questions about the mother’s mental state separate from physical recovery/baby's health.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: We spend so much time on the physical markers—the heart rates, the growth charts, the blood pressures—but there’s almost no room for the internal, invisible work.

innovation_opportunities.description: Peer-support facilitation programs specifically for 'high-information' parents to redirect research-compulsion into constructive, real-world community building.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: She saved me from my own search history.

expectations.text: I wanted autonomy in the decision-making... I wanted continuous labor support, delayed cord clamping, skin-to-skin immediately after.
I thought my background... would make me calmer. Instead, it's made me hyperaware of everything that could go wrong.
I imagined a provider saying, 'Here's what we're seeing, here's what might help, what do you want to do?'

perceptions.text: It felt more like a medical procedure was about to happen to me than like I was about to birth my child.
They placed her on my chest while the cord was still pulsing. I remember just... crying... I felt seen.
It felt like decisions happened to me. Completely.

valued_items.text: There was one moment... when a nurse just... held my hand while I cried. Didn't say much. Just was present.
There was this one pediatrician who explained everything she was doing as she did it.

missing_items.text: I needed someone to say, 'Recovery is going to be harder than you think, and that's normal. Here's what to expect, and here's when to call us.'
Nobody asked if I was okay emotionally.

service_gaps.expectation: Partner in decision-making and collaborative care.
service_gaps.perception: Compliance-based care where questions were treated as being 'difficult' or 'inconvenient'.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Training in trauma-informed, collaborative communication for clinical staff.

service_gaps.expectation: Holistic postpartum support addressing physical and emotional recovery.
service_gaps.perception: Rushed, fragmented care focusing only on basic physical healing and lacking mental health screening.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrated mental health triage into the 6-week postnatal checkup.

innovation_opportunities.description: Evidence-based, personalized postpartum recovery transition tool to manage expectations.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I needed someone to say, 'Recovery is going to be harder than you think, and that's normal.'

innovation_opportunities.description: Proactive emotional health 'check-in' protocol for postpartum visits.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Nobody asked if I was okay emotionally.

innovation_opportunities.description: Continuity-of-care model that bridges OB, mental health support, and pediatrician.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: No one coordinated between my care, the baby's pediatrician, maybe a mental health screening.

expectations.text: I need providers who understand that asking for the 'why' behind every decision isn't about not trusting them. It's about me taking back some control
I wanted minimal intervention unless medically necessary. I wanted to be informed before any procedure, not after.
I needed him to be present without needing me to manage his emotions at the same time

perceptions.text: She didn't rush me with explanations — when I asked about the fetal monitor strips, she showed me what she was looking at
You don't need to worry about that. Let me do my job.
The lactation consultant came in the hospital — once, briefly. She showed me a hold, said things looked 'fine,' and left.

valued_items.text: The nurse... didn't treat my questions like problems to solve. She just listened and adjusted things — the bed position, the lights
I think having that [online community] for first-time mothers over forty. That's been helpful

missing_items.text: None of them really knew me. They didn't know about the loss, not deeply.
I wish someone had just said, 'Fed is fed, and you're doing great.'
Nobody's asking me to process what happened in that room.

service_gaps.expectation: Participation in decision making based on provided data
service_gaps.perception: Patient told to just 'let me do my job' when questioning data
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Standardized collaborative decision-making protocol that treats patient expertise as an asset.

service_gaps.expectation: Continuous, holistic care post-birth
service_gaps.perception: Total disconnect; door closed on midwife-patient relationship
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Warm-handover transition programs where the birth attendant facilitates the first postnatal check.

service_gaps.expectation: Support for partner involvement
service_gaps.perception: Partner felt like a bystander; no guidance provided to him
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Partner-specific briefing tool or app that translates clinical environment and suggests emotional support roles.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: None of them really knew me. They didn't know about the loss, not deeply.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Nobody's asking me to process what happened in that room.

innovation_opportunities.description: Partner-training simulation or briefing cards to define roles during labor beyond medical observation.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Nobody really prepared him for what his role could be.

expectations.text: I thought if I just... documented it all, made it clear what I needed, the staff would see me as prepared, thoughtful.
I thought I'd be this calm person methodically working through pain management strategies.
I expected maternity care to see me as a whole person, not just a medical risk category.

perceptions.text: My midwife was there, and she was incredible... She held my hand during contractions, she talked to me between them.
The lactation consultant made me feel judged, like I was doing it wrong... They treated my fumbling like a symptom.
The staff wasn't unkind, exactly. But there was this... distance. Like they were processing me, checking boxes.

valued_items.text: My midwife was my one anchor. She was the one bright spot, honestly.
She [midwife] just... stayed with me. Said things like, 'You're doing so hard, you're so brave.' Stuff I needed to hear.

missing_items.text: I needed someone to reassure me that my anxiety didn't make me broken... Nobody explicitly said that to me.
I wish someone had just said, 'Here's why we can't do the tub right now,' you know? Instead of me guessing.

service_gaps.expectation: Expectation of being a partner in care and choosing pain relief methods methodically.
service_gaps.perception: Decision-making felt urgent, managed by staff, and swayed by her mental health label.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Structured shared decision-making aids that account for anxiety-prone scenarios.

service_gaps.expectation: Expectation of being treated as an individual, not a 'medium risk' clinical case.
service_gaps.perception: Felt constantly monitored and judged as a 'problem case' due to mental health history.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Trauma-informed care training for all staff (nurses/consultants) to de-pathologize mental health history.

innovation_opportunities.description: Trauma-informed breastfeeding support service that prioritizes maternal emotional well-being and confidence over technical perfection.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Nobody just... let me figure it out. Let me be clumsy and uncertain without it being pathologized.

innovation_opportunities.description: A 'Warm-Handoff' postpartum check-in system that moves beyond clinical vitals to address holistic mental health concerns specifically for high-risk individuals.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Nobody asked about my mental state in a way that felt real. Nobody said, 'We see you're struggling, here's what we can do.'

expectations.text: I was really clear about wanting to feel in control, because feeling out of control is where my anxiety takes over.
I wanted to be the person this baby needs.

perceptions.text: The OB barely acknowledged my mom was there. She was just focused on the medical stuff.
Sarah... she didn't make it about my mental health, she just helped me through it. In that moment, I felt capable.
It's like once they discharged me, their job was done... I was drowning, and nobody was watching for that except Sarah.

valued_items.text: Sarah checked in... That meant everything.
I needed someone to say, 'You're doing great... because they actually see me trying.'

missing_items.text: There was no counselor or social worker who's sat down and said, 'Okay, let's think through what this looks like for your mental health.'
I wish I had someone who could just be present without all that weight behind it... Someone who wasn't measuring whether I'm okay or not.

service_gaps.expectation: Wanted minimal intervention to stay in control.
service_gaps.perception: Felt decided for by the medical team due to anxiety diagnosis.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Develop decision-aid tools for neurodivergent/anxious patients that separate clinical risks from agency-preserving options.

service_gaps.expectation: Ongoing support for mental well-being.
service_gaps.perception: Systemic discharge after physical check, leaving patient isolated.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrated 'warm handoff' to mental health specialists as a standard part of the discharge process.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I needed someone to... just be present without all that weight behind it.

expectations.text: I thought I’d be able to breeze past the generic pamphlets and get straight to the nuanced clinical discussions.
I wanted guidance that felt like a partnership.
I expected some kind of proactive 'pre-conception counseling' that felt, I don’t know, personalized.
I really expected that same kind of relationship [continuity of care] here. I wanted a primary midwife who would see me, Heike.

perceptions.text: Being a nurse doesn't actually shield you from the vulnerability of being a patient; if anything, it makes it sharper.
The apps and websites I’ve stumbled upon are polarized: they’re either patronizing... or they’re alarmist, clinical databases.
The reality was much more transactional. It was cold, clinical, and rushed.
I see whoever is available on the schedule. One visit it’s a midwife I’ve met once before; the next, it’s someone completely new.

valued_items.text: I crave someone to just sit with me and acknowledge that being forty-two is hard.
A single, consistent point of contact—someone who knows my file, knows my story.

missing_items.text: What’s missing is a space where I don’t have to be the 'expert.'
There’s no space for the 'gray' areas. Nobody is saying, 'Heike, the data shows this, but here is how we are going to look after you as a person.'
There was no 'special' support—no extra time carved out to talk about the triggers [from previous loss].

service_gaps.expectation: Professional, peer-to-peer nuance in clinical communication.
service_gaps.perception: Automated, script-based bedside manner that ignores personal context.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Provider training in 'Advanced Maternal Age' psychosocial nuances.

service_gaps.expectation: Continuity of care with a named provider.
service_gaps.perception: Revolving door of strangers requiring constant re-explanation of history.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrated digital patient story dashboard.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want to talk to my midwife about the NIPT and the statistical realities, but then I catch myself wanting to stop, grab her hand, and just have her tell me that this baby is going to be okay.

innovation_opportunities.description: Digital 'bridge' tool that allows patients to record their personal history (including past loss) which is then automatically summarized for any provider viewing the chart.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’m not building a relationship; I’m just narrating my own file to a stranger.

innovation_opportunities.description: Peer support group platform specifically for healthcare professionals navigating pregnancy to reduce the 'expert' burden.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’m lonely in my own expertise. I’m surrounded by people who trust my brain so much that they’ve forgotten I still have a heart that’s racing, too.

expectations.text: I just want someone to tell me this is going to be okay.
I want to be a person, not a chart.

perceptions.text: He was very clinical, very 'data-driven.' I walked out of that office feeling like a collection of data points rather than a person.
She acknowledges the data—she knows the stakes... but she frames it within the context of me as a person who wants this baby.

valued_items.text: She didn't talk at me; she talked with me. She acknowledged the irony... She validated my anxiety without making it feel like a diagnosis.
She’ll sometimes just pause, look at me, and ask, 'Heike, how are you doing today, not your chart?'

missing_items.text: Every time I get a generic, automated email from the hospital system about 'advanced maternal age' screenings, it’s like a slap in the face.
I felt pushed toward decisions... without anyone asking how I felt about the anxiety those procedures would trigger.

service_gaps.expectation: Partner-in-care relationship where clinical expertise is respected but emotional human needs are met.
service_gaps.perception: System treats her as a high-risk data point and 'geriatric' case study due to age.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Need for person-centered clinical scripting that integrates emotional check-ins before risk data.

innovation_opportunities.description: Human-centric clinical communication training for providers to move beyond 'risk-first' language with older mothers.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: If someone starts their advice with 'Well, at forty-two, the statistics suggest...' they’ve lost me.

innovation_opportunities.description: Personalized digital health portals that allow users to toggle between 'Data-Dense' and 'Support-Focused' notification settings to reduce anxiety.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Every time I get a generic, automated email... it’s like a slap in the face. It feels so dehumanizing.

innovation_opportunities.description: Integrated 'Doula-led' support for clinical appointments to act as an emotional anchor and translator for medical bureaucracy.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: She’s been invaluable in helping me understand what I’m actually entitled to ask for.

expectations.text: I thought recovery would be about exhaustion, maybe some stitches, and just learning to navigate those first few weeks of sleepless nights with a newborn.
I really wanted that [bonding time]. I figured it would be natural—maybe a little tricky at first, but something my body and the baby would just know how to do.
I expected a space where I could be human... someone to say, 'That was terrifying, you went through hell, let’s make sure you’re okay—not just your blood pressure, but you.'

perceptions.text: They’d look at my chart, frown, and talk about me, not to me.
The nurses would come in and ask, 'Are you breastfeeding?' and when I’d say I was struggling, they’d give me this standard, clinical advice... like I was a machine that needed to be calibrated.
The check-ups felt like a conveyor belt... It was all about the 'data.' If my numbers were in range, they acted like I was 'recovered.'

valued_items.text: What I actually needed was someone to look at me, really look at me, and see that I was terrified.
I needed someone to be a human being with me.

missing_items.text: I’d mandate a 'transition of care' that isn't just about blood pressure or physical healing... Someone who stays with you for those first few months.
I’m still struggling with this deep sense of betrayal. My body felt like a traitor... I’m mourning the experience I thought I was supposed to have.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected care to be a bridge guiding her through the transition to motherhood and psychological validation.
service_gaps.perception: Received transactional clinical monitoring focused on charts and vitals with no space for trauma processing.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement trauma-informed postnatal follow-up pathways.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected breastfeeding support to be relational and intuitive.
service_gaps.perception: Received mechanical, box-ticking clinical instructions that increased feelings of failure.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Develop peer-support or specialized mental health-integrated lactation counseling.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: There should be a dedicated advocate—someone who isn't just rushing to the next appointment—who stays with you for those first few months.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a 'Trauma-Informed Postpartum Dashboard' that tracks qualitative mental health markers alongside clinical vitals.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want a system that validates that you’ve survived something hard, and stays to help you find your footing again.

expectations.text: I had this picture in my head of it being this quiet, bonding time, especially since I felt so disconnected from my body after the trauma.
I just kept thinking, I’ve done this before, I know how it goes.

perceptions.text: It felt like I was being watched from the second I walked into the clinic... I wasn’t Kelly anymore; I was just a list of risks and genetic probabilities.
The lactation consultants? They were relentless... It was like they were judging my worth as a mother by how many ounces I could produce.
It didn't feel continuous; it felt like I was a broken item being passed from department to department for 'assessment.'

valued_items.text: I craved just one person who would sit here, drink coffee, and not look at me like I was a ticking time bomb
She just acknowledged the war I was fighting inside. It was just a second, but it made me feel real again.

missing_items.text: Nobody asked me how I was doing while I fed him; they only cared about the mechanics of the feeding itself.
I wish someone would ask me, 'How is your heart doing?' instead of 'What’s your blood pressure today?'

service_gaps.perception: Clinical pressure and judgment regarding feeding mechanics
