check-ins, reassurance, concrete help navigating feeding and sleep, and someone noticing if I was unraveling

perceptions.text: My prenatal visits felt almost celebratory; I was the 'informed' patient who’d planned everything.
Follow-ups were medical and efficient but short on emotional assessment and practical support.

valued_items.text: safe delivery (which I did have)

missing_items.text: craving not advice but simple assurance that I was enough.
The biggest gap was emotional and practical postpartum support tailored to someone parenting alone.

service_gaps.expectation: someone noticing if I was unraveling
service_gaps.perception: Follow-ups were medical and efficient but short on emotional assessment
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Proactive psychosocial screening tools during standard medical follow-ups.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implementation of a 'Postpartum Social Reality' assessment tool that moves beyond physical vitals to screen for social isolation and emotional capacity in single-parent households.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I expected care that recognized the social realities behind the clinical notes

innovation_opportunities.description: Establishment of a 'Warm Handoff' system where postpartum clinicians are prompted to offer targeted emotional check-ins based on identified high-risk social factors (e.g., lack of home support).
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I remember the pediatrician asking if I had help at home and feeling my throat close up; I hadn’t admitted how lonely I was.

expectations.text: I expected to be back to baseline, or at least functional, pretty quickly because I 'knew' the process.
I thought my identity would just expand. I envisioned being this 'nurse-mother'—this seamless integration where my professional expertise would just bolster my confidence as a parent.
I thought [check-ups] would be the one place where I could set down the heavy burden of 'knowing,' where someone would finally ask, 'How are you doing, really?'

perceptions.text: They treated me like a pair of hands that needed a little calibration, rather than a human being whose nervous system was completely frayed.
The appointments felt like peer reviews... They checked my vitals, but nobody checked my spirit.
They filled my head with clinical details, but they starved me of the permission to be fragile.

valued_items.text: I wanted someone to look past the chart and the medical history and see the girl who was terrified of the silence in the night.
I wanted a system that gives us permission to stop performing, to put the 'nurse brain' down, and to just be scared, tired, and held.

missing_items.text: I didn't need another person to tell me my technique was correct; I needed someone to say, 'You look exhausted, and it’s okay to be scared.'
I needed someone to pull me back to earth, not just critique my hold.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected professionals to acknowledge the human, emotional side of motherhood.
service_gaps.perception: Professionals treated her as a clinical peer, focusing only on technical 'how-to'.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'human-first' clinical screening protocols that bypass professional labels.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected post-birth check-ups to be a safe harbor for emotional validation.
service_gaps.perception: Check-ups were sterile extensions of clinical work/peer reviews.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Standardize 'How are YOU?' check-ins in postnatal care pathways.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'Peer-Support-Neutral' screening tool for healthcare workers in maternity care that explicitly mandates emotional health triage.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want a system where the 'professional' label on my chart isn’t a signal to disengage, but an invitation for deeper support.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create 'Non-Clinical Support' pods where postnatal women can interact with mentors who hold space without giving medical advice.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I don't want to be a colleague; I want to be held.

expectations.text: I feel like I have to keep performing this role of the perfect, capable nurse-mom... I feel like I'm being forced back into a role that I'm not sure I can play anymore.
I want someone to look at me and ask how I am doing, not how I’m managing the clinical tasks.

perceptions.text: The care I received… it was technically perfect. My midwives were skilled, everything went by the book. But there was this distance.
They see the nurse, so they assume the mind is as controlled as the chart.

valued_items.text: She looked at me—not at the chart, not at the baby—and asked, 'Catrice, how are you holding up?'
She didn't start with the latch or the position. She started by asking me what I missed about my life before the baby.

missing_items.text: There was no continuity of care for me as a person. I felt like a set of vitals being handed off.
I’m twenty-three. I’m tired in a way that sleep doesn't fix... nobody really asked... in a way that invited an honest answer.

service_gaps.expectation: Expectation of being treated as a vulnerable new mother needing support.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a clinical expert/colleague who does not require emotional check-ins.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Provider bias training and standardized psychological screening for healthcare-professional patients.

service_gaps.expectation: Integrated, holistic support through the transition from birth to home.
service_gaps.perception: Fragmented, siloed check-ins focused exclusively on physical milestones.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Case-managed care pathway that includes social/emotional support for high-risk/professional mothers.

innovation_opportunities.description: Provider-to-Patient Psychological Safety Protocol: A formal screening tool for mothers who are healthcare professionals to acknowledge 'learned trauma' and shift focus from clinical expertise to emotional wellbeing.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Stop asking us what we think the protocols should be and start asking us how we’re holding up in the quiet.

innovation_opportunities.description: Respite Care Service for New Mothers: A service model where specialized support (like the private lactation consultant) is tasked with 'holding the baby' to provide the mother mandatory, guilt-free personal recovery time.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: She insisted I go for a twenty-minute walk... she wouldn't call me if anything happened.

innovation_opportunities.description: Cross-Specialty Continuity Dashboard: A shared digital care record that tracks more than vitals, including social context and prior emotional history, to prevent patients from 'starting from page one' with every new provider.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I had to explain my nursing background to every single person I met, over and over.

expectations.text: I expected that because I knew the "how-to" from a technical standpoint, the actual bonding and the physical rhythm would just snap into place.

I expected those check-ups to be a sanctuary. I honestly thought that once I was on the "patient" side of the desk, I’d be treated with the same gentle, comprehensive care I’ve seen my own mentors provide.

perceptions.text: My OB and the nurses see my chart, see my credentials, and they just shift into this professional shorthand. They assume I already know everything.
The appointments were brisk, efficient, and almost exclusively focused on the baby’s milestones. When I’d try to bring up how anxious I was feeling... it was like the air went out of the room.

valued_items.text: I needed someone to sit with me and just be a human. I needed someone to look at me and say, "You’re doing a great job, and it’s okay to be scared."

missing_items.text: I was treated as a chart to be cleared, not a person to be held. They gave me a checklist for my body, but they left me entirely alone with my mind.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected professional care and human connection due to clinical expertise.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a colleague/peer, leading to detached communication and lack of emotional validation.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implementing 'Human-First' protocols for healthcare professionals-as-patients.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected postnatal check-ups to address mental and emotional well-being.
service_gaps.perception: Appointments were clinical, 'brisk,' and exclusively focused on infant physiological milestones.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Standardized psychosocial assessment tools that trigger at-home check-ins.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'Peer-Patient Advocate' model where healthcare professionals receive support from a designated advocate whose role is to de-clutter the medical, professional identity and foster vulnerability.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’d want a model where patients—especially those like me who might be perceived as "experts"—are routinely assigned a dedicated advocate who isn't there to check vitals or sign charts, but to check in.

innovation_opportunities.description: Redesign risk-assessment profiles to include social risk factors (e.g., solo-parenting, high-stress professional backgrounds) as triggers for additional mental health support.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want a system that acknowledges the social risk factors as part of the clinical profile. Being a single mother, or having a high-pressure career in medicine, shouldn't be invisible variables.

expectations.text: I had this professional expectation that they would have some continuity of care, but it was just transactional.
I just want to feel normal.

perceptions.text: The nurses and doctors, they treat me like one of them, using this professional shorthand. They skip the basic, gentle reassurances that I see them give other moms.
The lactation consultants at the hospital were lovely, but they saw 'Cherri the Nurse,' not 'Cherri the overwhelmed new mom.'

valued_items.text: That lack of 'professional' scrutiny was the most refreshing thing I’d felt in weeks. I realized then how much I’d been craving someone to just see me as a person, not a chart.
She just stayed on the line with me, in silence, for twenty minutes. That was the first time in months I felt like I could actually breathe.

missing_items.text: They didn't stop to ask if I was okay, or if I needed a break.
I’m tired of being the person who knows the answer... I wanted someone to sit me down and just say, 'Cherri, you're the patient today.'

service_gaps.expectation: Professional care continuity and holistic support as a patient.
service_gaps.perception: Transactional, clinical shorthand approach that ignored maternal mental health needs.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'Patient-First' protocols for healthcare workers to de-escalate clinical professional expectations.

service_gaps.expectation: Emotional support acknowledging the vulnerability of new motherhood.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a 'colleague' or 'expert' rather than a mother in need of reassurance.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Training for staff to identify and consciously drop professional jargon when caring for clinician-parents.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a peer-support network specifically for healthcare workers navigating the postpartum period to allow for 'radical, messy, unfiltered honesty'.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I found a small, niche group of other medical professionals... That space was a godsend.

innovation_opportunities.description: Redesign postnatal intake processes to explicitly prompt clinicians to 'step out of the scrubs' and act as the patient, bypassing standard assumptions of medical knowledge.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wanted someone to sit me down and just say, 'Cherri, you're the patient today. You don't have to be the nurse.'

expectations.text: I expected a clean, clinical recovery... I’ll be back to myself in a few weeks.
I thought I’d be the one demonstrating [breastfeeding] for everyone else.
I thought my clinical background would act as a buffer [against postpartum mental health issues].
I expected [appointments] to be the moments where I’d finally be allowed to exhale... someone who would look past my chart and see that I was struggling.

perceptions.text: The reality? It was jarring... I felt like I was wearing a stranger's body.
I felt like I was being graded... I find myself giving these perfectly articulated summaries of her health... while I’m screaming inside.
The appointments felt like being on the other side of a glass wall... they were incredibly transactional.

valued_items.text: I needed them to see me not as a colleague... but as a new mother who needed permission to be vulnerable.
I didn't want a consultant; I wanted someone to hold my hand and tell me I wasn't doing it wrong.

missing_items.text: I just wish one person had looked at me and reminded me that I’m allowed to be human, even if I’m a nurse.
They checked my physical recovery—which was fine—but they never really checked me.

service_gaps.expectation: Medical professionals as partners who see beyond the chart/credentials.
service_gaps.perception: Transactional visits that validate the 'nurse' identity but ignore the 'mother's' emotional needs.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Provider communication training for healthcare workers who are also patients.

service_gaps.expectation: Self-recovery via clinical knowledge and stoicism.
service_gaps.perception: Severe anxiety, isolation, and an 'identity collision' between professional and personal roles.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Peer support groups specifically for healthcare professionals during the postpartum period.

innovation_opportunities.description: Provider-Patient Communication protocol for medical professionals-as-patients to de-escalate clinical jargon.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They speak to me in medical terms... which shuts down any space for me to just say, 'I’m scared, and I don't know what I'm doing.'

innovation_opportunities.description: Healthcare worker-specific postpartum support circles to provide psychological safety.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’m scared that if I asked a 'dumb' question, they’d look at me like I’d lost my mind.

expectations.text: I thought being a pediatric nurse would make this transition easier. I really did.
I expected some of them [colleagues] to reach out, maybe drop off a meal or just check in.

perceptions.text: The hospital lactation consultants were kind, but they treated me like a colleague... moving on to the next room before I could tell them that my brain was paralyzed by the pain.
My obstetric provider was professional, but those postpartum visits are so hyper-focused on physical recovery... the emotional landscape feels like an afterthought.

valued_items.text: She didn't ask about my vitals or my birth plan. She just gave me thirty minutes of peace.

She looked at me—not at the baby, not at the charts, not at the 'Nurse Alycia' version of me—and she said, 'You look like you're holding up the sky... You don't have to be the one who knows everything today.'

missing_items.text: Nobody has really sat me down and asked, 'Alycia, who are you in all of this?'
The continuity was totally gone. In the clinic, I was a patient, but at home, I’m an island.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional healthcare providers recognize the need for holistic support for all new mothers regardless of their clinical background.
service_gaps.perception: Providers assume clinical background equals emotional stability and skip standard supportive inquiry.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Provider sensitivity training for HCP-parents

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Human-First' screening protocol for healthcare workers in maternity care that explicitly separates clinical role from personal emotional state.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I just want the people in charge of maternity care to ask the 'nurse' to step aside for a moment.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create safe, moderated peer-support groups for healthcare professionals navigating the transition to parenthood to discuss vulnerability without fear of professional judgment.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Those communities offer something my textbooks and clinical rotations never did: the permission to be flawed.

expectations.text: I imagined my doctor would sit with me, really explain things. Not just the clinical facts, but... the wonder of it all.
I expected to feel grounded, but instead I feel like I'm suspended in something uncertain.
I expected to find answers. Clear, reassuring information about what to expect, how to prepare my body, what milestones to watch for.

perceptions.text: The appointments are fifteen, maybe twenty minutes. The nurse asks her questions... The doctor comes in... we're done. In and out.
The biggest surprise? How... dismissive it felt when I brought up my anxiety.
The positive surprise? That she scheduled the appointment at all. That someone was willing to see me before conception.

valued_items.text: The diagrams of fetal development—those are beautiful... I can trace the formation of the heart... It feeds something in me.
I need someone to see that I'm anxious about invisible things—the things I can't control. That I need education, yes, but also... presence.

missing_items.text: I need community. I need that more than anything else.
No one proactively offered me counseling, or a support group... No one said, 'It sounds like you're isolated—let me connect you with other women.'

service_gaps.expectation: Expectation of unhurried, holistic conversations about the emotional reality of pregnancy.
service_gaps.perception: Clinical checklist-driven appointments that dismiss anxiety as an inconvenience.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'emotional readiness' assessments as part of the standard preconception intake.

service_gaps.expectation: Expecting to find clear, curated, and comforting guidance from healthcare professionals.
service_gaps.perception: Drowning in chaotic, contradictory online information without professional synthesis.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Create a clinical-curated digital hub that bridges scientific facts with psychological coping strategies.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a facilitated peer-support group specifically for immigrant women navigating pregnancy without local family networks.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need community. I need that more than anything else.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a 'Whole-Person' intake tool that includes screens for cultural displacement, isolation, and anxiety, triggering a referral to social support services.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need healthcare providers to see beyond the numbers on a chart.

innovation_opportunities.description: Digital platform feature that allows 'shared journey' tracking, enabling partners to view clinical updates and educational content simultaneously to reduce individual burden.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: What I expected was a shared journey. What's actually happening is that I'm carrying most of the emotional weight myself.

expectations.text: I thought once we decided, I'd just feel... excited.
I want that support network, that wisdom from the women around you.

perceptions.text: The OB-GYN is... competent. She asked the right questions, did the right tests.
The interaction felt so transactional, you know? ... I felt like a checkbox on her list.

valued_items.text: That moment [the nurse] lasted maybe ten seconds, but it meant more than the whole appointment.

missing_items.text: I wish someone would just... sit with me in this uncertainty. Not solve it, just acknowledge it's real.
I wish there was a way to connect with other women here in Needham who understand what it's like to be starting this journey without your mother's hand to hold.

service_gaps.expectation: Needs emotional validation for anxiety/fears regarding pregnancy.
service_gaps.perception: Doctor treated her as a 'low risk' checklist and handed her a pamphlet.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'whole-person' intake assessments that screen for psychosocial isolation alongside clinical risk.

innovation_opportunities.description: Localized, facilitated peer-support circles for immigrant women to replicate missing family networks.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wish there was a way to connect with other women here in Needham who understand what it's like to be starting this journey without your mother's hand to hold.

innovation_opportunities.description: Training for clinicians in 'empathetic documentation'—integrating patient narratives and cultural context into clinical visits.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wish my OB had sat with me for ten more minutes and let me talk about what neural tube development means to me.

expectations.text: I imagined going to a doctor and being able to say, 'Hey, I'm thinking about starting a family... What should I know?' And I thought they'd actually want to know about my health, my lifestyle, my concerns.
I thought I'd feel more in control... having a plan, having the money saved, having a partner lined up.

perceptions.text: The nurse was nice enough, but she just kind of rattled off the standard stuff—take prenatal vitamins, don't smoke, blah blah blah.
When I started asking more specific questions about my cycle and timing, she seemed a little... dismissive? Like I was overthinking it.

valued_items.text: I felt empowered by that information [from apps/studies], like I was taking control of something I could actually control.
I needed someone to actually listen... acknowledge that my concerns are real, even if they're not medical emergencies.

missing_items.text: I needed someone to help me untangle all of this—the medical stuff and the emotional stuff and the practical stuff.
I need someone to acknowledge that this isn't just about biology for me. It's about whether I can afford to be a mother at all.

service_gaps.expectation: Expectation of a collaborative, holistic conversation about family planning.
service_gaps.perception: Dismissive, checkbox-driven interaction focused only on medical 'standard' advice.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement structured, non-clinical 'preconception coaching' sessions.

service_gaps.expectation: Expectation of professional validation of fertility tracking data.
service_gaps.perception: Healthcare providers ignored personal data, dismissing the patient as 'overthinking'.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Digital health integration that allows patients to upload personal health tracking data to clinic EHRs.

innovation_opportunities.description: Integrated 'Social & Reproductive Health' navigator who assists young/low-income patients in assessing stability, finances, and emotional readiness.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Someone who could help me figure out if this is actually the right time, or if I'm just scared of losing the opportunity.

innovation_opportunities.description: Validated digital support tools that offer 'contextualized' medical information to reduce anxiety and separate clinical data from social media noise.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: What I actually needed was context. Someone to say, 'Yes, this information is accurate, but here's what it actually means for your life.'

expectations.text: I came to a clinic visit with all my charts, all my data, thinking maybe they'd see how much I cared, how prepared I was.
I was ready to have a real conversation about my fertility, my timeline, what I should be doing.

perceptions.text: She glanced at them for maybe thirty seconds and just... set them aside. She told me I was young, healthy, no reason to worry, and that I should just relax
The online communities I mentioned — they're supportive but also kind of... intense? There's this undercurrent of urgency and fear.

valued_items.text: I want someone — a doctor, a healthcare provider, someone — to actually see me... To see that I'm serious and scared and doing everything right.
I was trying to take control of the one thing I could control.

missing_items.text: She didn't ask me why I was tracking so carefully. She didn't ask about my financial situation or my timeline or my fears.
The isolation of it all. That's the thing I wish someone would ask about. Not just whether I'm tracking my cycle correctly, but whether I have support.

service_gaps.expectation: Recognition of proactive data-tracking as a valid, serious health management strategy.
service_gaps.perception: Dismissal of patient data as 'flaky' or 'obsessive' behavior due to age-based bias.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Standardized intake protocols that ingest patient-generated data (e.g., cycle logs) to inform clinical conversation.

service_gaps.expectation: Holistic assessment addressing personal, financial, and emotional context.
service_gaps.perception: Purely biological, dismissive advice focused only on 'relaxing'.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Preconception health screening tools that incorporate social determinants of health and patient-identified stressors.

innovation_opportunities.description: Digital integration tool allowing patients to upload/sync cycle data directly into Electronic Health Records prior to appointments.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I walked in there with months of data... I'd been meticulous. And I printed it all out... She glanced at them for maybe thirty seconds.

innovation_opportunities.description: Preconception Support Peer-Mentoring or Counseling Service to bridge the gap for those lacking family/partner support.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: The isolation of it all... I wish someone would ask... whether I have someone to talk to about my fears.

innovation_opportunities.description: Bias-mitigation training for clinicians regarding age and socio-economic assumptions in reproductive health.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: She just saw a twenty-two-year-old and assumed I was overthinking.

expectations.text: I expected a partnership. I think I had this naive vision of walking into an office, sitting down with a provider who would look at my chart—and my history—and say, 'Okay, Deidre, we know this is heavy, let’s build a map together.'
I thought the healthcare system would treat my preconception phase as a deliberate, thoughtful preparation period.

perceptions.text: The reality has been… clinical. It’s efficient, sure, but it’s so transactional. When I show up for these visits, I’m usually exhausted... My providers see the 'medium risk' label and start talking about blood pressure... I’m just another appointment block.
It was just a series of 'clearances.' It felt less like preparation and more like a series of gates I had to pass through to prove I was viable.

valued_items.text: I need someone to look me in the eye and say, 'I know you're terrified. I see that you've been here before, and it’s okay to not be okay today.'
I’m asking for someone to sit in the dark with me while I’m waiting for the light to turn on.

missing_items.text: I think what’s missing is a space where I’m allowed to be a person, not a patient or a professional.
There’s no acknowledgment of the trauma of my past loss. It’s all about the future baby and the current test results.

service_gaps.expectation: Personalized, history-informed roadmap for pregnancy preparation.
service_gaps.perception: Transactional, 'clearance-based' medical appointments focused solely on risk metrics.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Develop a 'Patient Narrative Summary' tool that integrates past loss trauma into the EHR for providers.

service_gaps.expectation: Community-based support that balances data with empathy.
service_gaps.perception: Apps offering either toxic positivity or cold, clinical 'poppyseed' metaphors.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Create a 'dual-mode' digital health platform allowing users to toggle between clinical data and trauma-informed, empathetic content.

innovation_opportunities.description: Trauma-informed patient intake/onboarding process that prioritizes emotional history alongside medical history.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: When I walked back in, it was like hitting a 'reset' button. I was just a new patient with a code in my chart. No one asked how I was processing the trauma of the last time.

innovation_opportunities.description: Virtual 'Case Manager' advocate service to assist patients with high-risk profiles in navigating logistics and paperwork.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’ve had to be my own case manager... I’m the one pushing for specific screenings because I’ve read the literature... I shouldn't have to be.

expectations.text: We were going to be this team, this unit.
I’m ready to talk, but please, just… bear with me if I need a minute.

perceptions.text: I’m just a set of variables that were currently trending in the right direction.
The clinical interactions have been a mixed bag, but they mostly leave me feeling like a data point rather than a person.
That tiny bit of humanity felt like a lifeline.

valued_items.text: She didn't ask about my clinical history or my risk factors; she just asked if I’d had a chance to get a decent breakfast, and she said it with actual warmth.
I wish he’d just hold my hand and tell me it’ll be okay, regardless of what the science says.

missing_items.text: I just wish there was a space in the system for people like me... who aren't 'bad enough' to warrant a human touch.
I need a witness. I need someone to see that even though the blood work says I’m 'fine,' I’m carrying a backpack full of ghosts.

service_gaps.expectation: Needs emotional validation for history of loss during clinical visits.
service_gaps.perception: Clinical staff focus exclusively on blood work/metrics and ignore emotional state.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrate 'psychosocial screening' or 'trauma-informed check-ins' as a mandatory clinical step.

service_gaps.expectation: Expecting to feel like a person being cared for by a medical team.
service_gaps.perception: Feeling like a data point on a conveyor belt.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Adopt 'Relational Continuity' models where patients have a dedicated navigator for non-clinical emotional support.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implementation of a 'Psychosocial Navigator' role for medium-risk patients to bridge the gap between clinical appointments and emotional well-being.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need a witness. I need someone to see that even though the blood work says I’m 'fine,' I’m carrying a backpack full of ghosts.

innovation_opportunities.description: Trauma-informed communication training for clinical staff to ensure they acknowledge patient fears before diving into data.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wanted her to just acknowledge the fear... Instead, she looked at her tablet, tapped the screen, and said... Let's focus on the upcoming scan.

expectations.text: I expected—or maybe hoped is more honest—that this practice would do something similar. I know it's not always realistic, but I thought at least I'd see the same person most of the time, build some kind of relationship.
I expected—I mean, I hoped—that if something showed up on a screening, they'd sit down with me. Like actually sit down. Have the conversation face-to-face, maybe with my husband there.

perceptions.text: Instead, I've seen five different people in four months. Five. I had to tell my pre-eclampsia story to at least three of them because it wasn't always in their notes.

The reality was... I got a phone call. A phone call from someone in the office—not even my regular doctor. She was reading off notes... didn't pause to see if I understood. Didn't ask if I had questions.
She came in and before she did anything, she sat down. Like, actually pulled up a chair and sat at eye level with me. And she asked, 'How are you doing with all of this?'

valued_items.text: She [Sarah] came in and before she did anything, she sat down... And she asked, 'How are you doing with all of this?' Not 'How are your symptoms?' but how am I doing.
He knew my name without looking at the chart. He asked about my kids, remembered details about my first delivery. There was this thread of trust running through everything.

missing_items.text: Not a single person has asked me how I'm doing with the trauma of my last delivery... Nobody said, 'That must have been terrifying, and it makes sense that you're anxious now.'
Nobody gave me a clear roadmap. I got scattered pieces from different doctors, different visits... nobody ever sat down and gave me actual guidance.

service_gaps.expectation: Face-to-face, empathetic communication for screening results.
service_gaps.perception: Phone calls from strangers reading notes in a cold, matter-of-fact manner.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Standardize 'Difficult Conversation' protocols that require in-person or video consultations with a known provider for abnormal screenings.

service_gaps.expectation: Consistent provider relationship to build trust and history.
service_gaps.perception: Fragmented care with multiple providers leading to repetitive trauma re-telling.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement a 'Primary Midwife/OB' model for high-risk patients to ensure one clinical lead regardless of practice size.

innovation_opportunities.description: Patient-Facing 'Trauma-Informed' Digital Care Plan: A tool that tracks not just medical risks but emotional milestones and trauma history, accessible by all providers before the appointment.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I'm a person who's been through something. I needed someone to say: 'I see that you're scared. That's valid. Your fear matters.'

innovation_opportunities.description: Integrated Birth Trauma Support Pathway: Proactive screening for previous birth trauma with automated referrals to specialized counselors embedded in the maternity care workflow.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I never got closure. I went home with a baby... and this lingering sense that my body had betrayed me... nobody connected those dots.

expectations.text: I wanted someone who had seen pre-eclampsia before, who knew how to handle it.
What made me trust that GP before was that she treated me like a person, not a problem.

perceptions.text: The specialist made it sound very matter-of-fact... I left that room feeling like a folder full of test results instead of a person.
Dr. Chen... she listens when I'm scared, doesn't just brush it off as anxiety.
The practice just scheduled me for whatever appointments they thought I needed, and I just showed up. It's all just... happening to me rather than with me.

valued_items.text: Having someone see you and remember you.
My husband... he sits right there next to me. During the ultrasounds, he holds my hand.

missing_items.text: What I'm missing most is just... someone who sees me, not just my complications.
I wish someone had explained my rights, like, what I could push back on or question.
There was no conversation about how I was feeling about my body after that trauma.

service_gaps.expectation: Partner involved as a shared decision-maker in medical consultations.
service_gaps.perception: Husband present but often deferring to her or excluded from initial high-risk strategy discussions.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Develop joint decision-making tools for high-risk couples.

service_gaps.expectation: To be treated as a whole person/patient rather than just a clinical case.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a 'folder full of test results' and a 'vessel that might break'.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement trauma-informed care protocols in MFM settings.

innovation_opportunities.description: Trauma-informed care protocols for MFM specialists to ensure clinical communication doesn't alienate the patient.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I left that room feeling like a folder full of test results instead of a person.

innovation_opportunities.description: Integrated 'Patient Resource Navigator' or Doula support specifically for high-risk patients to bridge the gap between clinical appointments.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I don't even know if there are childbirth education classes specifically for high-risk pregnancies... nobody at the practice has ever handed me a resource list.

innovation_opportunities.description: Pre-conception 'emotional health' consults that address past trauma and establish a mental health plan alongside medical monitoring.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: There was no conversation about how I was feeling about my body after that trauma.

expectations.text: I expected to be treated as a partner in my care, but instead, I’m just a chart entry.
I think I foolishly expected a 'team' approach. You know, the kind of care where the provider asks, 'How are the kids?' or remembers that I was worried about my iron levels.

perceptions.text: It feels colder than I remembered. There’s no room in those fifteen-minute slots for the life happening outside the office.
The reality is... it’s mostly just a portal notification. I get this ping on my phone, and there’s a string of numbers that I have to interpret for myself.

valued_items.text: I want a provider who realizes that I’m not just a 'low-risk pregnancy'—I’m a woman who is terrified of the silence because I don't have a safety net.
A coworker dropping off a bag of groceries without making a big deal out of it, or a friend who just sits with me on the couch to watch a movie without asking about the baby or the finances.

missing_items.text: Everything is missing, to be honest. It’s a total disconnect. On a practical level, nobody has ever asked, 'How are you getting here?' or 'How does this appointment time work with your job?'
I wish they’d stop asking me about 'anxiety' as if it’s a symptom to be suppressed and start asking me about my life as a factor that influences my health.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected a partnership where technical knowledge is valued and conversation is bidirectional.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a passive patient; information delivered via cold portal pings with no time for questions.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'shared visit planning' tools that allow patients to prioritize their specific questions/life stressors prior to the visit.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected a consistent team who remembers personal context (the 'whole person').
service_gaps.perception: A revolving door of providers requiring the patient to recite her own medical history at every visit.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Patient-centric dashboard summary that highlights personal/social determinants alongside clinical vitals.

innovation_opportunities.description: Integrate Social Determinants of Health (SDOH) into intake scripts to move beyond clinical metrics.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wish they’d stop asking me about 'anxiety' as if it’s a symptom to be suppressed and start asking me about my life as a factor that influences my health.

innovation_opportunities.description: Virtual/Flexible asynchronous care pathways to support patients with work/transport barriers.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: How can I possibly afford fresh produce... or how do I manage extra monitoring appointments when I don't have the flexibility at work?

expectations.text: I thought 'being in charge' meant proving I was the most informed patient in the room. I wanted them to treat me like a colleague, someone they could talk to as an equal.
I believed that if I walked into an office, the person in the white coat was fundamentally on my side.

perceptions.text: My doctor—she’s technically great at her job. She’s efficient, she’s quick, and she never misses a clinical beat.
The system makes it incredibly hard, honestly. The scheduling is rigid, the portals are confusing... nobody explains the financial reality.
It’s transactional... there’s no room in those fifteen-minute slots for me to be anything other than a textbook example.

valued_items.text: I need someone to look at me and ask how I’m actually managing with three kids and a bank balance that’s perpetually hanging by a thread.
I want them to stop pretending this is just a medical event... I want to feel like I’m in charge because I’m respected.

missing_items.text: There’s nobody there to catch the things I’m too tired to process. When the doctor talks about risks or testing, there’s no one to hold my hand.
I’m the translator, but I’m translating for myself... sometimes I catch myself wishing I could just sit down with a community health worker.

service_gaps.expectation: To be treated as a peer and expert participant in care.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a 'logistical hurdle' and a 'subordinate' to be processed.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Provider communication training on acknowledging patient background/expertise.

service_gaps.expectation: Holistic care that accounts for social stressors (finances, parenting).
service_gaps.perception: Strict focus on clinical vitals and 'textbook' metrics.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integration of social determinants of health (SDOH) screening into routine appointments.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'social/life check-in' protocol that precedes clinical measurements to validate the patient's holistic reality.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need someone to look at me and ask how I’m actually managing with three kids and a bank balance that’s perpetually hanging by a thread.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a financial transparency tool or 'financial navigator' role to provide real-time cost transparency for procedures before they are ordered.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Nobody explains the financial reality of these appointments until you’re already standing at the front desk with a bill in your hand.

innovation_opportunities.description: Offer 'solo-patient' support modules or assigned patient advocates for unpartnered individuals to fill the 'witness/advocacy' gap.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Being alone means I have to fight twice as hard to be taken seriously... it’s an incredibly heavy, invisible burden.

expectations.text: I thought it would feel more... official, I guess. Like, I'd walk in and there would be this whole team... and they'd lay out this comprehensive plan.
I expected... maybe a sit-down conversation? Like, someone would review the results with me point-by-point, explain what each number meant.
I expected one person... that I'd have one midwife who'd see me through everything, who'd know my story.

perceptions.text: The reality was... smaller. Which sounds like it should be better, but it made me feel smaller too.
What actually happened was my midwife called me. On the phone... And then... that was it.
She's not always available. Sometimes I call and she's at another clinic... I'm back to square one.

valued_items.text: My midwife asked me early on what I wanted my birth to look like. Like, not in a clinical way — she genuinely asked what I envisioned.
He [my dad] shows up in these small ways, and it makes me feel less alone than I thought I'd be.

missing_items.text: I wish someone had just... acknowledged that gap [between clinical knowledge and living it].
I wish someone had just asked if I was okay. Not clinically — actually okay.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional, comprehensive review of test results.
service_gaps.perception: Transactional phone call notification with no space for questions.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Automated digital result portal combined with scheduled 'results review' debriefs for high-anxiety patients.

service_gaps.expectation: Consistent, personalized care from one provider.
service_gaps.perception: Fragmented care from on-call staff who do not know her history or emotional context.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrated digital patient passport that provides providers with 'emotional context' and 'preferences' instantly.

service_gaps.expectation: Rapid response and accessibility for concerns.
service_gaps.perception: Voicemail system with 24-hour turnaround in a remote area.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Triage-based secure messaging app or 24/7 midwifery telehealth line.

innovation_opportunities.description: Virtual prenatal classes designed for solo/vulnerable parents to participate without the barrier of physical isolation or 'couple-centric' environments.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I almost laughed. It's an hour away, and it's full of couples probably. I'd show up alone... so I didn't go.

innovation_opportunities.description: Emotional-clinical hybrid care pathway where 'medical status' is paired with an 'emotional well-being check' to avoid the 'perfect patient' trap.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: My midwife assumes I don't need emotional support because I understand the clinical side.

expectations.text: I thought that would feel less... medicalized, I guess. Less judgment.

perceptions.text: The clinic staff are efficient with paperwork and scheduling, but nobody's really *asked* me how I'm doing with any of this emotionally.
She's the one who tells me everything looks "textbook," and I do believe her more than I'd believe some doctor in a white coat, you know?

valued_items.text: She just... checks in. Asked how I was feeling last week when I looked really pale. Brought me ginger tea once without making it weird.
My midwife presented it really neutrally, which I appreciated. She didn't push either way.

missing_items.text: I think what's hardest to access isn't information exactly. It's guidance. Someone saying, "here's what you need to know, here's where to get it, here's how to ask for it."
I've thought about wanting to talk to someone about the anxiety, the isolation, all of it. But there's no therapist in Douglas.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected a less medicalized and judgmental environment.
service_gaps.perception: Felt judged by the staff's reactions to being young and unmarried; felt reduced to a clinical case number.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Staff training on unconscious bias and trauma-informed care for young, solo pregnant people.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected healthcare providers to guide her through rights and options.
service_gaps.perception: Left to navigate system, FMLA, and benefits entirely on her own without proactive support.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Proactive patient navigator or digital patient-rights portal.

innovation_opportunities.description: Virtual 'peer-support' or 'doula-matching' service for solo/young mothers to fill the emotional and non-clinical guidance gap.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I think what I need most is someone who doesn't make me feel like I have to apologize for my situation just by existing in the exam room.

innovation_opportunities.description: Digital 'Pregnancy Passport' app providing proactive checklists for rights, FMLA, and benefits to replace 'hacking it' alone.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Nobody proactively told me any of that... I've had to learn by doing. By showing up and seeing what happens.

expectations.text: I imagined cold, sterile rooms where I’d be treated like a case study, but I also thought that the clinical nature of it would be a safety net.
I envisioned a partnership—someone who would eventually become a familiar face, a person who would walk into the room, remember the context of my previous loss.
I thought because I understood the medical jargon, we’d be able to have a deeper, more sophisticated discussion about what the results meant.

perceptions.text: The reality was incredibly transactional. It was mostly 'The test results came back normal, see you in four weeks.'
The reality, though, has been a rotating door. Because I’m at a larger practice, I’m often shuffled between different midwives.
They see my knowledge as a source of stress, but they don't realize that my silence or my 'clinical' questions are actually a plea for them to acknowledge that my body has failed me before.

valued_items.text: I really crave that one person who knows that when I get quiet, it’s not because I’m calm—it’s because I’m scared.
That kind of quiet, non-technical presence is the only thing that’s actually helped me breathe lately.

missing_items.text: I need them to bridge the gap between the clinical, cold facts and the messy, terrified reality of being pregnant after a loss.
I want to be held in the sense of being listened to, not just managed by a protocol.

service_gaps.expectation: Personalized, meaningful dialogue about test results based on medical literacy.
service_gaps.perception: Transactional, 'check-the-box' delivery of normal results.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Context-aware reporting tools that integrate patient history (trauma/loss) into result delivery.

service_gaps.expectation: Long-term partnership with a consistent midwife.
service_gaps.perception: Rotating door of providers requiring repetitive re-explanation of trauma history.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Digital patient-profile 'briefing' system to ensure new providers are informed of trauma history before entry.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Trauma-Informed Care' flag in electronic medical records that prompts providers to ask about current emotional state/history of loss before clinical discussion.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want a provider to look at my chart, see the history of my loss... and say, 'I know today is just a routine check, but how are you holding the weight of this pregnancy?'

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a 'clinical bridge' tool for health-literate patients to discuss anxieties specifically related to their clinical knowledge, separating 'medical data' from 'emotional context'.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need someone to bridge the gap between the clinical, cold facts and the messy, terrified reality of being pregnant after a loss.

expectations.text: I thought if I, a trained educator, ever found myself in this position, I’d be the perfect, informed patient—one who could navigate the appointments with ease and feel secure because I knew exactly what was happening
I wanted a mentor, not just a practitioner. I wanted her to be the maternal figure who could finally tell me, 'Charline, you can let go of the textbooks now; I’ve got this.'

perceptions.text: She sees me as a source of information, not a person in need of comfort... It’s like she’s talking to a child, which makes me feel like I’m being dismissed rather than cared for.
My midwife is great, truly, but I feel this tension where I’m too smart for my own good.

valued_items.text: I crave that human connection—someone to just put a hand on my shoulder and say, 'Charline, you’re safe.'
I want someone to look at me and acknowledge that I’m carrying a heavy past, and that my 'clinical literacy' is actually just a desperate, scared mechanism

missing_items.text: I really wish the healthcare system had a way to recognize that 'low risk' on paper doesn’t mean 'low need' in reality.
I find myself wishing I had someone in my real life—a mother, an older friend—who could just tell me, 'Stop looking at the data and go eat something.'

service_gaps.expectation: Expected the medical system to recognize her as an informed partner and provide holistic safety.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a clinical data point and 'vessel to be monitored', leading to alienation.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'whole-person' intake screenings that capture non-medical history like past pregnancy loss.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Beyond-the-Chart' psychosocial intake process that specifically screens for past pregnancy loss and emotional support needs, regardless of clinical risk status.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I really wish the healthcare system had a way to recognize that 'low risk' on paper doesn’t mean 'low need' in reality.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a clinical support tool that encourages clinicians to move beyond 'simplified' language when encountering highly literate patients, instead focusing on emotional validation and 'holding space'.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: She sees me as a source of information, not a person in need of comfort.

expectations.text: I pictured warm, unhurried conversations where someone would look at me—not just my blood pressure cuff—and ask how I was doing
I expected the provider to sit down... and walk me through the numbers, explain the 'why' behind the tests
I naively believed in the 'midwife model' of care—you know, that romanticized idea of a dedicated relationship where the provider actually knows your name, your fears, and your story.

perceptions.text: It’s clinical, hurried, and frankly, a bit cold. The environment is sterile, and the appointments feel like a sprint
It’s a portal notification or a hurried summary while I’m still trying to pull my shirt back down. It’s impersonal.
There’s no continuity. I’m not a patient to them; I’m a series of appointments to be checked off.

valued_items.text: I need someone to help me bridge that gap—to say, 'We know you're struggling to get here; here is a transportation voucher,' or 'We know your job is demanding; here is a note that actually advocates for your need to rest.'
I need a provider who stops the clock for thirty seconds and just says, 'I see you. You’re handling a lot. You’re doing a good job.'

missing_items.text: Practical help is the most glaring void. It’s like they assume I can just manifest a ride to the lab or a healthy meal
There’s no context, no acknowledgement of the terror that comes with seeing a flag on your own chart.

service_gaps.expectation: Personalized, unhurried care and holistic partnership.
service_gaps.perception: Mechanical, hurried, and impersonal transactions.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'Relationship-based' scheduling where patients have a named primary midwife regardless of rotation.

service_gaps.expectation: Systemic support for logistical barriers (transport, finance).
service_gaps.perception: System ignores social determinants, labeling them as 'not my department'.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrate social prescribing/navigators within the maternity clinic to manage non-clinical barriers.

innovation_opportunities.description: Integration of 'Social Determinants of Health' (SDOH) screening and navigation into standard antenatal workflow.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need someone to help me bridge that gap—to say, 'We know you're struggling to get here; here is a transportation voucher'

innovation_opportunities.description: Human-centric digital result interface that provides context, 'so what' explanations, and emotional validation rather than raw data.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: There’s no context, no acknowledgement of the terror that comes with seeing a flag on your own chart.

innovation_opportunities.description: Peer-support facilitation program specifically for single mothers or those without a support network, curated by the clinic.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I find these anonymous spaces where I can read someone else’s post about being exhausted or scared... It’s solidarity in the trenches.

expectations.text: I pictured a mentor, maybe an older clinician I’d worked with, someone who would just know—someone who would look at my chart and say, 'Hedwig, I’ve got you, we’re going to figure out the logistics together.'

perceptions.text: I feel like I’m being pushed through a conveyor belt where the decision-making is already done before I even open my mouth.
My midwife is kind, truly, but there’s this invisible barrier between us.

valued_items.text: I’ve actually found a strange, quiet connection with the woman who drives the community shuttle sometimes... She’s just a person who notices when I’m struggling... It’s a tiny thread of recognition.

missing_items.text: I need someone who isn't a medical professional and isn't a stranger on a screen. I need a real, living, breathing person who doesn't need me to be the 'good patient.'
I need more than a clinical evaluation of my blood pressure; I need someone to help me map out how to get to the clinic when the bus doesn't run where I need it to.

service_gaps.expectation: Collaborative, logistical support for medical appointments.
service_gaps.perception: Bureaucratic, checklist-driven care that ignores external socio-economic barriers.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrate social determinants of health (transportation/financial) into the clinical intake process.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Social Determinants Liaison' role who co-creates care plans with patients to address transit and logistical barriers, moving beyond clinical monitoring.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need more than a clinical evaluation of my blood pressure; I need someone to help me map out how to get to the clinic.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a patient-provider 'Humanization Tool' for appointments where clinicians can record non-clinical life context (e.g., transport, housing stability) to avoid 'non-compliant' labels.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: She didn't ask, 'How are you getting here?' or 'Is there anything we can do to make this easier?' She just moved on to the next item on the checklist.

innovation_opportunities.description: Offer peer-to-peer or professional 'Pregnancy Companions' for solo patients to act as a witness and buffer in clinical settings, mitigating the power imbalance.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: If I had someone with me—even just for the long, nerve-wracking moments before the heartbeat starts—I think I could breathe.

expectations.text: I imagined a midwife who knew my history, who would sit with me, speak calmly through each contraction, hold my hand, remind me to breathe, and reassure me when my brain raced through worst‑case scenarios.
I expected care to be efficient and kind; what I needed was permission to be scared.

perceptions.text: There were moments of real kindness — one midwife stayed beside me, kept eye contact, and listened when I confessed how afraid I was. That held me together.

But too often care felt transactional: staff focused on vitals and tasks, conversations were rushed or happened over my head, and I had to repeat details about my pre‑eclampsia and preferences as if they were new.

valued_items.text: When clinicians actually asked me what I wanted and then wrote it down, I felt steadier — like I had a seat at the table instead of being an emergency variable.
A midwife who acknowledged my fear instead of smoothing it away — it changed everything, even for a moment.

missing_items.text: Shift changes made continuity vanish.
Postpartum follow-up has been a weak spot: clearer BP check schedules, a defined person to call about concerns, and emotional support or screening for postpartum anxiety.

service_gaps.expectation: Consistent midwife who knew her history.
service_gaps.perception: Having to repeat details about pre-eclampsia and preferences during every shift change.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Digital patient summary dashboard available in-room for incoming staff.

service_gaps.expectation: To be a partner in decision-making.
service_gaps.perception: Decisions presented as edicts or inevitable plans (e.g., magnesium therapy).
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Mandatory structured time for questions after presenting a treatment plan.

innovation_opportunities.description: Digital bedside clinical summary that tracks preferences and clinical history to prevent repetitive questioning during shift changes.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: A printed plan or a quick in-room huddle helped when it happened.

innovation_opportunities.description: Postpartum 'bridge' service that provides clear BP monitoring schedules, specific point-of-contact, and mental health screening.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Postpartum follow-up has been a weak spot: clearer BP check schedules, a defined person to call about concerns, and emotional support

expectations.text: I imagined walking into these appointments feeling... I don't know, held? Like I'd be treated as someone who understood the medical landscape.
I expected... clarity, I guess. We'd prepare patients, contextualize the numbers, give them space to process.
I thought... that someone would... step in. Take ownership of my case, you know?

perceptions.text: The information I get is solid but surface-level.
What I got is a rotating door... I'm re-explaining things.
The medical care itself is competent. Dr. Patterson knows her medicine.

valued_items.text: It's honestly nice to be asked to just talk about it, rather than having someone run through a checklist.
The support that actually helps is the unexpected, small stuff. The casseroles. The listening.

missing_items.text: Nobody asked me how I'm actually doing. Nobody acknowledged that I went from being the person explaining these things to being the person living them.
I needed someone to say, 'This is scary. It's okay to be scared.'
I thought maybe there'd be social work support, resources for rural patients managing complicated pregnancies.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected to be treated as a peer who understands the clinical landscape.
service_gaps.perception: Treated with simplified, surface-level explanations.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'Expert Patient' intake flags to tailor communication levels.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected continuity of care to feel held and known.
service_gaps.perception: Experienced a 'rotating door' of providers without knowledge of patient history.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Digital patient health passports or consistent anchor-midwife models for high-risk patients.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected a middle ground for urgent versus routine concerns.
service_gaps.perception: System dictates either wait a week or go to the ER.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Virtual triage/tele-consultation lines staffed by clinicians who can interpret vitals to reduce unnecessary ER travel.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a peer-support network specifically for former medical professionals navigating high-risk pregnancy.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I'm sitting alone with my knowledge, unable to really talk to anyone about what I'm actually scared of.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a 'Financial and Logistical Navigator' role for rural patients requiring frequent specialist visits.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: There's no discussion of that in these appointments. No acknowledgment that 'monitor yourself carefully' is easy to say when you're not isolated in rural Massachusetts.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a clinical communication bridge that acknowledges patient expertise to reduce patient-provider anxiety.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want to say, 'I know what edema is,' but then I'd have to explain everything, and that changes the dynamic somehow.

expectations.text: I need to be the patient sometimes.
What I really miss is having someone who could bridge both worlds. Someone who could talk to me as someone who understands the science, but who also sees me as scared.

perceptions.text: Dr. Chen—she's been wonderful. Really wonderful. She's patient, thorough, and I can tell she actually listens when I come in with concerns.
The maternal-fetal medicine specialist I see... Sometimes I feel like I'm defending myself against him, which is exhausting when I should just be trusting.
Dr. Chen gave me the illusion of choice, but it wasn't really a choice. She presented two options like they were equally valid, when we both knew one was objectively safer.

valued_items.text: The continuity with Dr. Chen matters most because she's consistent.

missing_items.text: I wish I had a doula. Someone trained, someone who understands both the medicine and the emotional piece.
Nobody from the healthcare system helped with [insurance and logistics]. I had to do the detective work myself.

service_gaps.expectation: Needs someone to bridge clinical knowledge with emotional empathy.
service_gaps.perception: Treated either as a colleague (no emotional support) or as a generic patient (patronizing).
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Training providers on 'advanced patient' personas.

service_gaps.expectation: Genuine shared decision-making regarding care options.
service_gaps.perception: Presenting options without acknowledging financial/circumstantial constraints.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrated socio-economic intake screening tools.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'Care Navigator' role for high-risk patients to bridge clinical/logistical gaps (gas, childcare, insurance).
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Nobody from the healthcare system helped with that. I had to do the detective work myself.

innovation_opportunities.description: Provider communication training for patients with medical backgrounds to allow 'dual-mode' dialogue (clinical + emotional validation).
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: What I really miss is having someone who could bridge both worlds. Someone who could talk to me as someone who understands the science, but who also sees me as scared.

innovation_opportunities.description: Telehealth and remote patient monitoring integration to reduce rural travel burden and financial strain.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Every appointment means driving forty minutes... coordinating childcare, worrying about the gas money.

expectations.text: I thought people would actually talk to me — like, sit down and explain why my belly is doing what it’s doing, what’s normal, what to watch for.
I wanted gentle exams, someone who’d say, “It’s okay to worry,” and not act like I was being dramatic.
I expected a midwife who knew my name, my job schedule, and that I stay up reading about SIDS at night.

perceptions.text: The first few visits felt clinical and fast — checklists, measurements, “low-risk” said almost like a mic drop.
Some providers were kind and explained kicks or Braxton Hicks; others did quick belly checks without much context.
Pelvic exams felt awkward and rushed sometimes; I left feeling a little embarrassed instead of reassured.

valued_items.text: Some providers were kind and explained kicks or Braxton Hicks
someone who’d say, “It’s okay to worry”

missing_items.text: I often had to push for detail. It made me more determined to learn everything myself, which is exhausting.
a midwife who knew my name, my job schedule

service_gaps.expectation: Midwife who knows my history and personal life.
service_gaps.perception: Fast, clinical, checklist-driven appointments.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'named midwife' primary care models or digital longitudinal profiles that providers must review pre-appointment.

service_gaps.expectation: Detailed explanations of physical changes and validation of concerns.
service_gaps.perception: Quick checks without context, leaving patient to Google.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Digital patient portal with visual trackers for 'normal' body changes and symptom guides to reduce reliance on generic internet searches.

innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I expected a midwife who knew my name, my job schedule, and that I stay up reading about SIDS at night.
